content.content:
1	[normal] FR-DATA-5: Ledger and record ingestion shall be source-agnostic, so simulated data can be replaced by lawfully-provided real data without redesign.
2	[Heading 3] Projections & Explainability
3	[normal] FR-PROJ-1: Scenario arithmetic shall execute in a deterministic sandbox, never as free-form LLM math.
4	[normal] FR-PROJ-2: Every projection / finding shall render the four-tier audit trail (Premise, Parameterization, Equation, Grounding).
5	[normal] FR-PROJ-3: Parallel scenarios (e.g., Bull vs. Bear) shall run in isolated state contexts with no cross-contamination.
6	[Heading 3] Evaluation & UI
7	[normal] FR-EVAL-1: The system shall log RAGAS metrics continuously and surface them in the engineer panel.
8	[normal] FR-EVAL-2: Concept/data-drift monitors shall flag when the index needs rebuilding.
9	[normal] FR-EVAL-3: Optimization changes (quantization or engine swaps) shall trigger an automated regression test before adoption.
10	[normal] FR-UI-1: The UI shall provide a Financial Analyst Workspace and a Platform Engineer Control Panel.
11	[Heading 1] 6. Non-Functional Requirements
12	[normal] NFR-PRIV-1 (Privacy): No confidential/MNPI data shall leave the host. Public/synthetic data may be sent to external providers only under explicit opt-in and only via the data-classification gate; MNPI shall never appear in logs or URLs.
13	[normal] NFR-PRIV-2 (Cache isolation): Prefix-cache reuse shall be isolated per session (e.g., via cache_salt) to prevent cross-session inference.
14	[normal] NFR-PERF-1 (Latency): With a warm cache, interactive chat shall target sub-second time-to-first-token (to be calibrated on target hardware).
15	[normal] NFR-PERF-2 (Decoupling): Heavy ingestion shall not degrade interactive latency (enforced by the async/sync split).
16	[normal] NFR-CORR-1 (Correctness): Numerical projections shall be deterministic and reproducible from the recorded equation and inputs.
17	[normal] NFR-OBS-1 (Observability): Every agent step, tool call, cache hit-rate, and self-correction iteration shall be inspectable.
18	[normal] NFR-RES-1 (Resource bounds): The system shall run within a defined local hardware budget; edge deployment via 4-/8-bit quantization.
19	[normal] NFR-MAINT-1 (Portability): Inference engines and providers shall be interchangeable behind an OpenAI-compatible interface; tools behind MCP contracts.
20	[normal] NFR-GOV-1 (Governance): All external routing shall be policy-enforced, logged, and auditable; the default posture is local-only.
21	[normal] NFR-PROV-1 (Provenance): Every external/web-sourced claim shall carry source and timestamp, distinguishable from internally-grounded evidence.
22	[Heading 1] 7. Success Criteria & Acceptance Tests
23	[normal] The MVP is accepted when the following are demonstrable:
24	[normal] Discrepancy detection: Given a 10-K asserting a liability of $X and a simulated ledger summing to $Y ≠ $X, the system flags the delta as a high-risk finding, citing both the source quote and the exact SQL used.
25	[normal] Grounded explainability: The finding renders all four audit-trail tiers and the grounding quote is traceable to the source filing.
26	[normal] Self-correction: When RAGAS faithfulness/relevance fall below threshold, the loop reformulates and improves the score on retry (observable in traces).
27	[normal] Zero egress: A network test confirms no outbound calls to external inference/data endpoints during a full audit.
28	[normal] Regression safety: An inference-optimization change (quantization, or a vLLM ↔ SGLang swap) is gated by an automated regression test asserting no degradation in reasoning/math accuracy.
29	[normal] Projection determinism: Re-running a saved scenario reproduces identical numbers from the recorded equation and inputs.
30	[normal] Provider benchmark: The suite produces a head-to-head report of the sovereign model vs. at least one external provider on the public corpus (faithfulness, math/SQL accuracy, latency, cost).
31	[normal] Egress governance (negative test): An attempt to route MNPI-tagged content to an external provider is blocked and logged.
32	[normal] Web-grounded forecast: A forecast incorporates a current public web fact with correct provenance (URL + timestamp) in the audit trail, while the filing/ledger remains the primary basis.
33	[normal] Staged training comparison: The training ladder reports per-capability scores for base, SFT-only, distillation-only, and SFT-then-distillation against the provider baseline, and the stopping rule selects the lowest-complexity rung that clears every capability bar without regressing any other.
34	[normal] Graceful data scaling: Run on the minimal data set, the Accounting facet produces verified findings while Legal/Operational/Compliance are reported as claims-only; when an evidence layer is added, the corresponding facet automatically becomes evidence-backed and the coverage summary updates — with no code change.
35	[normal] Compound, mixed-granularity retrieval: A question spanning two sections at different granularities returns a summary from one node and a span from another, both provenance paths appear in the audit trail, and the coverage check confirms every sub-question is addressed before generation.
36	[Heading 1] 8. Guiding Principles (Engineering Constitution)
37	[normal] These principles are binding constraints for all downstream specs, plans, and implementation tasks. Where a design decision conflicts with a principle, the principle wins.
38	[normal] P1 — Local-first, confidential zero egress: Confidential/MNPI data never leaves the host. External providers are permitted only for public/synthetic data under an explicit, governed opt-in (benchmarking and distillation), enforced by the data-classification gate.
39	[normal] P2 — Determinism over guesswork: All arithmetic runs in a sandbox; LLMs never perform multi-step math directly.
40	[normal] P3 — Grounded & explainable: Every material claim cites a source; provenance comes from the semantic retrieval layer, never from an efficiency mechanism.
41	[normal] P4 — Measure before optimizing: Build the simplest mechanism first (e.g., CAG Tier 1); add complexity (Tier 2, sparsity) only on observed evidence.
42	[normal] P5 — Honest mechanics: Capabilities are described as they actually behave (best-effort vs. guaranteed); no overstated “zero-latency / locked-in” claims.
43	[normal] P6 — Self-correction by default: Generation is wrapped in evaluation loops that catch and repair low-quality output.
44	[normal] P7 — Uniform contracts: Engines sit behind the OpenAI-compatible API; tools sit behind MCP — no brittle, hardcoded integrations.
45	[normal] P8 — Regression-gated change: No optimization ships without passing the regression suite.
46	[normal] P9 — Privacy by construction: Per-session cache isolation; no sensitive data in logs, URLs, or shared caches.
47	[normal] P10 — Web data augments, never overrides: Real-time web signals add timely context to forecasts, but the filings and ledger remain the primary source of truth; all external evidence is provenance-tagged and conflicts are surfaced, not silently resolved.
48	[normal] P11 — Data-adaptive operation: Aegis runs on the minimal data set and expands to holistic four-facet coverage as evidence layers are added. A facet without its evidence layer is reported as claims-only or not-assessed — never silently treated as verified — and every output carries a coverage/confidence summary.
49	[Heading 1] 9. Assumptions, Dependencies & Constraints
50	[Heading 3] Assumptions
51	[normal] Target 10-K / 10-Q filings are publicly available via SEC EDGAR.
52	[normal] A bounded but GPU-capable local host is available for server-side inference; analyst edge devices are CPU / low-VRAM.
53	[normal] A simulated ledger is acceptable for development and evaluation in place of real MNPI.
54	[Heading 3] External Dependencies
55	[normal] SEC EDGAR API; open-weight base models and their licenses (e.g., Qwen3, GLM-5.1, DeepSeek V4, Phi-4); vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp; a model router (e.g., LiteLLM); training stack for SFT/distillation/DPO (e.g., PEFT/LoRA-QLoRA, TRL/Axolotl); a teacher model for distillation (a frontier provider or large open model); external provider API keys (External mode only); agent-native web search providers (e.g., Exa, Tavily, Firecrawl, Valyu); LangGraph; RAGAS; a vector store (e.g., Qdrant); PostgreSQL; a Tableau-compatible BI target; Arize Phoenix / LangSmith; LMCache (Tier 2 only).
56	[Heading 3] Constraints
57	[normal] Fixed local hardware budget; no external network inference; deterministic explainability is mandatory, not optional.
58	[Heading 1] 10. Core Architecture & Data Strategy
59	[normal] Aegis is designed on an Asynchronous Ingestion / Synchronous Generation split. Long-running ingestion workers process incoming corporate files in the background, ensuring that when an analyst logs into the UI, the system provides sub-second model response times.
60	[Heading 2] 10.1 Data Strategy Matrix
61	[normal] To test the system thoroughly without relying on live, confidential corporate data, Aegis combines real-world public documentation with simulated internal records. The first four rows are the minimal/accounting baseline; the last three are progressive evidence layers that light up the remaining diligence facets (see Section 10.2).
62	[Heading 2] 10.2 Data-Adaptive Operation & Capability Tiers
63	[normal] Aegis is designed to run on whatever evidence is available and to expand gracefully toward the full four-facet audit as more data is supplied. The minimal configuration is always sufficient to operate; additional evidence layers enrich coverage rather than being prerequisites. Each facet has a claims layer (what the filing asserts) and an evidence layer (the records that verify it); the filings supply the claims layer for all four facets, while the evidence layers arrive progressively.
64	[normal] Tier 0 — Minimal (always available): 10-K/10-Q + simulated general ledger + regulatory rulebooks. The Accounting facet is fully functional (the claims-vs-ledger reconciliation gotcha). Legal, Operational, and Compliance are readable at the claims level from the filings, but explicitly marked unverified.
65	[normal] Tier 1 — + Operational tables: Customer/CRM, supplier, and HR tables enable the Operational facet (concentration, churn, attrition). Cheapest expansion — a Postgres schema extension consumed via the existing MCP.
66	[normal] Tier 2 — + Legal corpus: Contracts, leases, IP, and dockets enable the Legal facet (clause-level risk), consumed through the existing RAPTOR layer.
67	[normal] Tier 3 — + Compliance register: Licenses, certifications, and violation history enable the Compliance facet, pairing the register with the CAG rulebooks. All tiers present = full holistic coverage.
68	[normal] The operating contract: at any tier the system runs; a facet without its evidence layer is reported as claims-only (unverified) or not-assessed, never silently presented as reconciled. Every report and the analyst UI carry a coverage/confidence summary stating which facets are evidence-backed. Because the ingestion paths are uniform (documents → RAPTOR, structured records → Postgres MCP, rules → CAG), adding a facet is a data-onboarding step, not an architectural change.
69	[Heading 2] 10.3 Event-Driven Background Ingestion Pipeline
70	[normal] When a target asset document is added to the system, an event-driven worker runs an automated background ingestion flow:
71	[normal] [ New 10-K Document ] --> [ Structural Parser (ToC Tree) ] --> [ Per-Node Granularity Decision ] --> [ Within-Node RAPTOR Sub-Trees ]
72	[normal] |
73	[normal] v
74	[normal] [ User Report Trigger ] <-- [ LangGraph Multi-Agent Engine ] <-- [ Postgres SQL Validation Layer ]
75	[normal] Structural Parsing: Documents are broken down by headers, preserving layout syntax and embedding markdown tables cleanly alongside their associated prose.
76	[normal] Hierarchical Indexing: A structural table-of-contents tree is built from the document’s natural headings, and any oversized section is given a within-node RAPTOR sub-tree (summarize-or-split) — the structure-routed hybrid detailed in Module 2.
77	[normal] Cache Preparation: Long-term vector spaces and local key-value caches are pre-warmed before an analyst starts an interaction.
78	[Heading 1] 11. Technical Execution Modules
79	[Heading 2] 11.1 Module 1: Model Inference Optimization (The Muscle)
80	[normal] To run massive open-weight models (e.g., Llama-3-70B, Mistral-Large, or deep-reasoning variations) on local enterprise servers, Aegis utilizes low-level inference optimization frameworks. Server-side generation is handled by two co-primary engines; edge execution by a third.
81	[Heading 3] Server-Side Deployment (vLLM & SGLang — co-primary)
82	[normal] Both engines expose OpenAI-compatible APIs and share the same core throughput mechanisms (continuous batching and paged attention). They are deployed as interchangeable backends; SGLang is preferred for the prefix-heavy and structured-output paths.
83	[normal] vLLM (baseline concurrent serving): PagedAttention plus continuous batching, broad model coverage, and a mature OpenAI-compatible server. The default engine for general multi-agent concurrency.
84	[normal] SGLang (preferred for CAG + structured output): Adds RadixAttention, which holds the KV cache of all concurrent requests in a radix tree, enabling multi-level prefix sharing and automatic fork/branch sharing. This matches Aegis’s shape — a shared rulebook prefix and supervisor/system prompts reused across every sub-agent, and forked Bull/Bear scenario branches sharing a parent context. Its constrained-decoding path (XGrammar) is also substantially faster, benefiting the structured JSON audit trails.
85	[normal] TensorRT-LLM (optional performance overlay): NVIDIA-only compiled engine for maximum throughput/latency once the model and hardware are frozen. Adopted late: it carries significant setup cost (~1–2 weeks) and single-vendor lock-in, so it is not an iteration-phase engine.
86	[Heading 3] Shared Core Mechanisms (both server engines)
87	[normal] Continuous Batching (Iteration-Level Scheduling): Instead of standard request-level batching (which waits for all generations in a batch to complete, wasting GPU cycles on short requests), the engine uses token-level execution scheduling. New incoming agent requests are dynamically integrated into the active execution batch at iteration boundaries, significantly driving up concurrency throughput.
88	[normal] Paged Attention: Mitigates memory bottlenecks caused by the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache. By allocating physical KV cache memory in non-contiguous pages (similar to virtual memory paging in operating systems), Paged Attention sharply reduces KV cache fragmentation, allowing for larger effective context limits and multiple parallel agent operations on a single GPU cluster.
89	[Heading 3] Engine Interchangeability & Switching
90	[normal] Because both engines speak the OpenAI-compatible protocol, all LangGraph agents target a single endpoint and remain agnostic to the backend. Switching is therefore a configuration concern, not a code change, escalating as needed:
91	[normal] Deploy-time config (baseline): An environment/config value selects the live engine. This fis also how the vLLM-vs-SGLang comparison is run in the evaluation suite — flip the config, re-run the regression set, compare.
92	[normal] Gateway routing (flexible): A thin router (e.g., a LiteLLM-style proxy) routes per workload — prefix-heavy CAG traffic and structured-JSON generation to SGLang, the remainder to vLLM — behind one endpoint.
93	[normal] Constraint — no shared KV cache: The two engines do not share a KV cache; a prefix warmed on one is not reusable by the other. Routing must therefore be session-sticky (an audit session stays pinned to one engine) to preserve the prefix-cache hits the CAG design depends on.
94	[Heading 3] Edge / Client Deployment (llama.cpp & GGUF)
95	[normal] For mobile analysts operating on isolated hardware, models are compiled into GGUF format via llama.cpp.
96	[normal] Weights undergo 4-bit and 8-bit weight quantization (e.g., Q4_K_M and Q8_0 schemas), dropping local VRAM requirements dramatically while keeping perplexity loss minimal.
97	[normal] Development tooling note: Ollama (a convenience wrapper around llama.cpp) is used only for local prototyping and model smoke-testing. It is not a concurrent production serving engine and is never placed on the server path, which belongs exclusively to vLLM / SGLang.
98	[Heading 2] 11.2 Module 2: Advanced & Agentic RAG (The Knowledge)
99	[normal] Standard semantic search fails on complex financial audits because a single liability risk might be mentioned on page 12 and explained in a footnote on page 240, and because the most-similar chunk is not always the most relevant one. Aegis therefore uses a structure-routed hierarchical retrieval design — a hybrid of PageIndex-style reasoning navigation and RAPTOR-style summarize-or-split — backed by the cache-driven CAG path for static rulebooks.
100	[Heading 3] Retrieval Design: Structure-Routed Hierarchical Retrieval (PageIndex × RAPTOR)
101	[normal] Retrieval runs in two phases: structure decides where to look, and a within-node mechanism decides how much to return. This composition cures the characteristic weakness of each technique used alone — RAPTOR’s tendency to sever a fact from the context that explains it, and pure PageIndex’s overflow when a returned section is too large or the answer is a tiny span within it.
102	[normal] Phase 1 — Structural navigation (PageIndex-style routing). At ingestion, a “table-of-contents” tree is built from the document’s natural headings (e.g., Item 8 → Note 14 (Leases) → sub-notes), each node holding a title and a short summary. At query time an LLM reasons over that compact ToC — not the full text — to navigate to the correct node, following cross-references (“see Note 14”) as a human would. The output is a traceable path to the right section, explainable by construction.
103	[normal] Phase 2 — Within-node RAPTOR (summarize-or-split). A node does not store one monolithic blob of section text. At ingestion each section is given a granularity decision against a configured size threshold: if it is small enough to use whole, it is stored as-is; if it is oversized, a mini-RAPTOR index is built over just that section’s content (chunk into ~300-token leaves → embed → cluster → summarize into a small local sub-tree). Retrieval is then served by level — a global question about the section returns the node’s compact summary (which always fits), while a local needle question runs a vector search within that node’s leaves only and returns the precise span.
104	[normal] Global Queries (“What is this firm’s structural exposure to real estate leases?”) are answered from a node’s within-node summary — never by dumping the full section into context.
105	[normal] Local Queries (“What were the precise Q3 maintenance fees?”) are answered by a span search within the already-correct node’s leaves, pinpointing the sentence rather than returning the whole section.
106	[Heading 3] Compound Queries: Multi-Node, Mixed-Granularity Retrieval
107	[normal] A real audit question often spans several sections and needs a different granularity from each — the gist of one section and a pinpointed span from another (e.g., “What is the firm’s overall real-estate strategy, and what was the exact Q3 lease payment?”). The design treats this as the general case, not an exception: “global vs. local” is a property of each retrieved piece, not of the whole query. Navigation therefore decomposes a compound query and emits a set of typed instructions rather than selecting a single node.
108	[normal] Typed (node, intent) instructions: Navigation decomposes a compound query into sub-questions and returns a list of (node, intent) pairs — each target node tagged with the intent that selected it (global or local). The navigation step carries why each node was chosen, not merely that it was.
109	[normal] Per-instruction serving (mixed granularity is the norm): FR-RAG-4’s serve rule is applied to each instruction independently — a global-intent node returns its within-node summary; a local-intent node returns a within-node span search. A single answer may therefore combine a summary from one section and a span from another.
110	[normal] Assembly (budget, dedup, conflict): A composition step assembles the heterogeneous pieces into one context — allocating the token budget across blocks (how many spans per local node, demoting summaries when many sections are touched, when to stop adding nodes), deduplicating overlap, and flagging cross-node conflicts (e.g., a liability stated differently in the lease note vs. the tax note) for the self-correcting loop rather than resolving them silently.
111	[normal] Richer provenance, not messier: Because each piece carries its own navigation path, a mixed retrieval yields multi-source grounding — a strategy summary from Item 7 (MD&A) alongside a span from Item 8 → Note 14 — which is exactly the multi-source evidence a real audit finding needs.
112	[Heading 3] RAPTOR (the within-node mechanism)
113	[normal] Within an oversized node, RAPTOR (Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval) builds the local sub-tree bottom-up: ~300-token sentence-aligned leaves are embedded, soft-clustered using Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) (so a chunk relevant to two themes can feed both), and each cluster is summarized by a local LLM into a parent node; the summaries are re-embedded and re-clustered recursively up to a local root. The bottom layer holds fine detail (needle retrieval) and the upper layers hold abstraction (global summaries) — confined to the single section the navigation step selected.
114	[Heading 3] Why the composition is the chosen design
115	[normal] Cures severed context: Every leaf lives under its section node, reached by an explicit path, so a retrieved fact always carries its structural context (which section, which note) instead of floating free as in corpus-wide RAPTOR.
116	[normal] Bounds similarity error: Vector search runs only within an already-correct section — a small, on-topic pool — so “similar-but-wrong” matches are confined to a region the reasoning step already vouched for.
117	[normal] Keeps the hot path cheap: Navigation reasons only over the compact ToC (titles + summaries), and the expensive per-node sub-trees are built once during async ingestion. One light reasoning call plus a tiny local vector search is far cheaper than reasoning over full sections, preserving the interactive-latency target.
118	[normal] Native provenance: The navigation path is the audit trail — “Item 8 → Note 14 → [span]” drops directly into the four-tier explainability Grounding tier (Section 12). This is especially strong for the Legal facet, where a clause is navigated to by structure, the precise span is extracted, and the clause’s surrounding section remains intact for meaning.
119	[Heading 3] Caveats & mitigations
120	[normal] Navigation latency: The routing LLM call adds latency versus pure vector search; mitigated by keeping the ToC compact, caching navigation for recurring query shapes, and falling back to a within-corpus collapsed-tree vector search when a query is latency-critical.
121	[normal] Ingestion cost: Building both a ToC tree and per-node sub-trees is more work, but it is asynchronous and therefore fits the decoupled architecture.
122	[normal] Oversized-node threshold: An explicit, configurable threshold decides when a section gets a sub-tree; it should be calibrated, not hardcoded on intuition.
123	[normal] Atomic oversized leaves: A genuinely indivisible large unit (e.g., one dense table with no sub-structure) still forces a careful, table-aware split — the single place where the “no arbitrary chunking” ideal must bend.
124	[Heading 3] Cache-Augmented Generation (CAG) — Staged Implementation
125	[normal] For high-frequency, unchanging regulatory parameters (e.g., active GAAP accounting rules, IRS tax codes), Aegis avoids per-query vector lookups by keeping the rulebook resident as a precomputed KV state rather than retrieving it at runtime. This is implemented in two stages so that the simplest mechanism is built and measured first, with persistence added only when the data justifies it.
126	[normal] Tier 1 — Baseline (shared-prefix KV reuse). The regulatory corpus is ordered as a fixed, byte-identical prompt prefix placed ahead of all per-query content, so the engine’s shared-prefix cache reuses its KV blocks across requests — the rulebook is prefilled once and skipped thereafter. This is genuine CAG: no vector search, no re-encoding of the corpus. Both server engines support this mechanism: vLLM via Automatic Prefix Caching (APC), and SGLang via RadixAttention. SGLang is the preferred engine for this path — its radix-tree cache shares the rulebook prefix (and the common supervisor/system prompts) across all concurrent sub-agents and across forked scenario branches, which fits Aegis’s request shape more closely than APC’s per-request hash matching.
127	[normal] Honest performance characteristic: APC reduces time-to-first-token by eliminating repeated prefill over the rulebook. It is not “zero-latency” — attention is still computed over the cached tokens during decode. The win is the removed prefill, not free inference.
128	[normal] Honest reliability characteristic: APC is best-effort. Cached blocks are reclaimed under an LRU policy when GPU memory is contended, and the in-process cache does not survive an engine restart (a cold first query re-warms it). Under heavy multi-agent concurrency, the rulebook prefix can therefore be evicted and recomputed.
129	[normal] Cache isolation (privacy): Because Aegis handles MNPI, prefix-cache reuse is isolated per session. vLLM exposes a per-request cache_salt for this (only requests sharing the same salt may reuse cached blocks, closing the timing side-channel); whichever engine is deployed must enforce equivalent per-session cache isolation.
130	[normal] Tier 2 — Optional Upgrade (Persistent KV via LMCache). When monitoring shows the rulebook prefix is being evicted and recomputed under real load (see Section 13.1), the KV state is promoted to a persistent tier using LMCache, which provides multi-engine KV storage with tiered GPU→CPU→SSD offload. This guarantees residency across load spikes and process restarts and fits the bounded-hardware, on-prem profile: the rulebook KV lives in CPU/SSD and is loaded into GPU on demand rather than permanently occupying VRAM. Tier 2 is a measured enhancement, not a prerequisite.
131	[normal] Decision rule: Build Tier 1; instrument the rulebook cache hit rate in the Engineer Control Panel; adopt Tier 2 only when observed eviction/recompute of the regulatory prefix degrades latency. This Tier 1 → Tier 2 transition doubles as a concrete regression-suite exercise (before/after TTFT and hit-rate under induced load).
132	[Heading 3] Self-Correcting Evaluation Loop
133	[normal] Every document extraction pass runs inside an autonomous verification envelope using RAGAS metrics programmatically:
134	[normal] # Programmatic RAGAS Verification Envelope
135	[normal] faithfulness    = check_faithfulness(retrieved_context, generated_response)
136	[normal] answer_relevance = check_relevance(user_query, generated_response)
137	[normal] if faithfulness < 0.85 or answer_relevance < 0.80:
138	[normal] # Autonomous query reformulation block
139	[normal] new_query = reformulate_search(user_query, generated_response)
140	[normal] retry_retrieval_loop(new_query)
141	[normal] Because retrieval now plans and assembles across nodes, the loop distinguishes three failure classes and routes a different correction to each: a planning failure (decomposition missed a sub-question or mis-assigned its intent) → re-decompose; a navigation failure (routed to the wrong node) → re-navigate; an extraction failure (right node, wrong span or summary) → re-search within the node. Ahead of the faithfulness/relevance gate, a coverage check verifies that the assembled context actually addresses every sub-question the compound query was decomposed into — catching the planning failure that RAGAS faithfulness alone would miss.
142	[Heading 2] 11.3 Module 3: Advanced Agent Memory Management (The Brain)
143	[normal] An audit covers weeks of analytical iteration. Standard context windows quickly suffer from token explosion or information erasure. Aegis separates state mechanics into a multi-tiered memory architecture:
144	[normal] Working Memory: Stores the current task’s active execution-graph state dictionary within individual LangGraph nodes, tracking localized loop conditions.
145	[normal] Episodic Memory (Short-Term): A rolling context buffer that remembers recent conversational history, analyst adjustments, and intermediate tool responses.
146	[normal] Semantic Memory (Long-Term): A vector-space database that stores durable, verified analytical facts, past transaction insights, and corporate metrics across separate login sessions.
147	[normal] Asynchronous Memory Compaction: To keep token overhead minimal, a background compaction loop triggers when episodic memory reaches a set token limit. It processes raw conversational logs, converts them into flat semantic facts, updates the Semantic Vector store, and purges the redundant text transcripts from the active execution path.
148	[Heading 2] 11.4 Module 4: Multi-Data Source Integration via MCP (The Hands)
149	[normal] To prevent brittle, hardcoded database endpoints, Aegis uses Anthropic’s open Model Context Protocol (MCP) via standard JSON-RPC protocol engines. This allows agents to interact safely with external tools through a uniform contract.
150	[Heading 3] PostgreSQL MCP Server
151	[normal] Exposes secure, parameterized SQL tools to the multi-agent graph. This gives the agent structural system validation access to check corporate text filings against the transactional database.
152	[normal] /* Outgoing JSON-RPC MCP Tool Invoke Request */
153	[normal] {
154	[normal] "jsonrpc": "2.0",
155	[normal] "method": "tools/call",
156	[normal] "params": {
157	[normal] "name": "run_audit_query",
158	[normal] "arguments": {
159	[normal] "sql": "SELECT SUM(amount) FROM operating_ledger WHERE account_type = 'lease_deficit'"
160	[normal] }
161	[normal] },

[normal] FR-DATA-5: Ledger and record ingestion shall be source-agnostic, so simulated data can be replaced by lawfully-provided real data without redesign.
[Heading 3] Projections & Explainability
[normal] FR-PROJ-1: Scenario arithmetic shall execute in a deterministic sandbox, never as free-form LLM math.
[normal] FR-PROJ-2: Every projection / finding shall render the four-tier audit trail (Premise, Parameterization, Equation, Grounding).
[normal] FR-PROJ-3: Parallel scenarios (e.g., Bull vs. Bear) shall run in isolated state contexts with no cross-contamination.
[Heading 3] Evaluation & UI
[normal] FR-EVAL-1: The system shall log RAGAS metrics continuously and surface them in the engineer panel.
[normal] FR-EVAL-2: Concept/data-drift monitors shall flag when the index needs rebuilding.
[normal] FR-EVAL-3: Optimization changes (quantization or engine swaps) shall trigger an automated regression test before adoption.
[normal] FR-UI-1: The UI shall provide a Financial Analyst Workspace and a Platform Engineer Control Panel.
[Heading 1] 6. Non-Functional Requirements
[normal] NFR-PRIV-1 (Privacy): No confidential/MNPI data shall leave the host. Public/synthetic data may be sent to external providers only under explicit opt-in and only via the data-classification gate; MNPI shall never appear in logs or URLs.
[normal] NFR-PRIV-2 (Cache isolation): Prefix-cache reuse shall be isolated per session (e.g., via cache_salt) to prevent cross-session inference.
[normal] NFR-PERF-1 (Latency): With a warm cache, interactive chat shall target sub-second time-to-first-token (to be calibrated on target hardware).
[normal] NFR-PERF-2 (Decoupling): Heavy ingestion shall not degrade interactive latency (enforced by the async/sync split).
[normal] NFR-CORR-1 (Correctness): Numerical projections shall be deterministic and reproducible from the recorded equation and inputs.
[normal] NFR-OBS-1 (Observability): Every agent step, tool call, cache hit-rate, and self-correction iteration shall be inspectable.
[normal] NFR-RES-1 (Resource bounds): The system shall run within a defined local hardware budget; edge deployment via 4-/8-bit quantization.
[normal] NFR-MAINT-1 (Portability): Inference engines and providers shall be interchangeable behind an OpenAI-compatible interface; tools behind MCP contracts.
[normal] NFR-GOV-1 (Governance): All external routing shall be policy-enforced, logged, and auditable; the default posture is local-only.
[normal] NFR-PROV-1 (Provenance): Every external/web-sourced claim shall carry source and timestamp, distinguishable from internally-grounded evidence.
[Heading 1] 7. Success Criteria & Acceptance Tests
[normal] The MVP is accepted when the following are demonstrable:
[normal] Discrepancy detection: Given a 10-K asserting a liability of $X and a simulated ledger summing to $Y ≠ $X, the system flags the delta as a high-risk finding, citing both the source quote and the exact SQL used.
[normal] Grounded explainability: The finding renders all four audit-trail tiers and the grounding quote is traceable to the source filing.
[normal] Self-correction: When RAGAS faithfulness/relevance fall below threshold, the loop reformulates and improves the score on retry (observable in traces).
[normal] Zero egress: A network test confirms no outbound calls to external inference/data endpoints during a full audit.
[normal] Regression safety: An inference-optimization change (quantization, or a vLLM ↔ SGLang swap) is gated by an automated regression test asserting no degradation in reasoning/math accuracy.
[normal] Projection determinism: Re-running a saved scenario reproduces identical numbers from the recorded equation and inputs.
[normal] Provider benchmark: The suite produces a head-to-head report of the sovereign model vs. at least one external provider on the public corpus (faithfulness, math/SQL accuracy, latency, cost).
[normal] Egress governance (negative test): An attempt to route MNPI-tagged content to an external provider is blocked and logged.
[normal] Web-grounded forecast: A forecast incorporates a current public web fact with correct provenance (URL + timestamp) in the audit trail, while the filing/ledger remains the primary basis.
[normal] Staged training comparison: The training ladder reports per-capability scores for base, SFT-only, distillation-only, and SFT-then-distillation against the provider baseline, and the stopping rule selects the lowest-complexity rung that clears every capability bar without regressing any other.
[normal] Graceful data scaling: Run on the minimal data set, the Accounting facet produces verified findings while Legal/Operational/Compliance are reported as claims-only; when an evidence layer is added, the corresponding facet automatically becomes evidence-backed and the coverage summary updates — with no code change.
[normal] Compound, mixed-granularity retrieval: A question spanning two sections at different granularities returns a summary from one node and a span from another, both provenance paths appear in the audit trail, and the coverage check confirms every sub-question is addressed before generation.
[Heading 1] 8. Guiding Principles (Engineering Constitution)
[normal] These principles are binding constraints for all downstream specs, plans, and implementation tasks. Where a design decision conflicts with a principle, the principle wins.
[normal] P1 — Local-first, confidential zero egress: Confidential/MNPI data never leaves the host. External providers are permitted only for public/synthetic data under an explicit, governed opt-in (benchmarking and distillation), enforced by the data-classification gate.
[normal] P2 — Determinism over guesswork: All arithmetic runs in a sandbox; LLMs never perform multi-step math directly.
[normal] P3 — Grounded & explainable: Every material claim cites a source; provenance comes from the semantic retrieval layer, never from an efficiency mechanism.
[normal] P4 — Measure before optimizing: Build the simplest mechanism first (e.g., CAG Tier 1); add complexity (Tier 2, sparsity) only on observed evidence.
[normal] P5 — Honest mechanics: Capabilities are described as they actually behave (best-effort vs. guaranteed); no overstated “zero-latency / locked-in” claims.
[normal] P6 — Self-correction by default: Generation is wrapped in evaluation loops that catch and repair low-quality output.
[normal] P7 — Uniform contracts: Engines sit behind the OpenAI-compatible API; tools sit behind MCP — no brittle, hardcoded integrations.
[normal] P8 — Regression-gated change: No optimization ships without passing the regression suite.
[normal] P9 — Privacy by construction: Per-session cache isolation; no sensitive data in logs, URLs, or shared caches.
[normal] P10 — Web data augments, never overrides: Real-time web signals add timely context to forecasts, but the filings and ledger remain the primary source of truth; all external evidence is provenance-tagged and conflicts are surfaced, not silently resolved.
[normal] P11 — Data-adaptive operation: Aegis runs on the minimal data set and expands to holistic four-facet coverage as evidence layers are added. A facet without its evidence layer is reported as claims-only or not-assessed — never silently treated as verified — and every output carries a coverage/confidence summary.
[Heading 1] 9. Assumptions, Dependencies & Constraints
[Heading 3] Assumptions
[normal] Target 10-K / 10-Q filings are publicly available via SEC EDGAR.
[normal] A bounded but GPU-capable local host is available for server-side inference; analyst edge devices are CPU / low-VRAM.
[normal] A simulated ledger is acceptable for development and evaluation in place of real MNPI.
[Heading 3] External Dependencies
[normal] SEC EDGAR API; open-weight base models and their licenses (e.g., Qwen3, GLM-5.1, DeepSeek V4, Phi-4); vLLM, SGLang, llama.cpp; a model router (e.g., LiteLLM); training stack for SFT/distillation/DPO (e.g., PEFT/LoRA-QLoRA, TRL/Axolotl); a teacher model for distillation (a frontier provider or large open model); external provider API keys (External mode only); agent-native web search providers (e.g., Exa, Tavily, Firecrawl, Valyu); LangGraph; RAGAS; a vector store (e.g., Qdrant); PostgreSQL; a Tableau-compatible BI target; Arize Phoenix / LangSmith; LMCache (Tier 2 only).
[Heading 3] Constraints
[normal] Fixed local hardware budget; no external network inference; deterministic explainability is mandatory, not optional.
[Heading 1] 10. Core Architecture & Data Strategy
[normal] Aegis is designed on an Asynchronous Ingestion / Synchronous Generation split. Long-running ingestion workers process incoming corporate files in the background, ensuring that when an analyst logs into the UI, the system provides sub-second model response times.
[Heading 2] 10.1 Data Strategy Matrix
[normal] To test the system thoroughly without relying on live, confidential corporate data, Aegis combines real-world public documentation with simulated internal records. The first four rows are the minimal/accounting baseline; the last three are progressive evidence layers that light up the remaining diligence facets (see Section 10.2).
[Heading 2] 10.2 Data-Adaptive Operation & Capability Tiers
[normal] Aegis is designed to run on whatever evidence is available and to expand gracefully toward the full four-facet audit as more data is supplied. The minimal configuration is always sufficient to operate; additional evidence layers enrich coverage rather than being prerequisites. Each facet has a claims layer (what the filing asserts) and an evidence layer (the records that verify it); the filings supply the claims layer for all four facets, while the evidence layers arrive progressively.
[normal] Tier 0 — Minimal (always available): 10-K/10-Q + simulated general ledger + regulatory rulebooks. The Accounting facet is fully functional (the claims-vs-ledger reconciliation gotcha). Legal, Operational, and Compliance are readable at the claims level from the filings, but explicitly marked unverified.
[normal] Tier 1 — + Operational tables: Customer/CRM, supplier, and HR tables enable the Operational facet (concentration, churn, attrition). Cheapest expansion — a Postgres schema extension consumed via the existing MCP.
[normal] Tier 2 — + Legal corpus: Contracts, leases, IP, and dockets enable the Legal facet (clause-level risk), consumed through the existing RAPTOR layer.
[normal] Tier 3 — + Compliance register: Licenses, certifications, and violation history enable the Compliance facet, pairing the register with the CAG rulebooks. All tiers present = full holistic coverage.
[normal] The operating contract: at any tier the system runs; a facet without its evidence layer is reported as claims-only (unverified) or not-assessed, never silently presented as reconciled. Every report and the analyst UI carry a coverage/confidence summary stating which facets are evidence-backed. Because the ingestion paths are uniform (documents → RAPTOR, structured records → Postgres MCP, rules → CAG), adding a facet is a data-onboarding step, not an architectural change.
[Heading 2] 10.3 Event-Driven Background Ingestion Pipeline
[normal] When a target asset document is added to the system, an event-driven worker runs an automated background ingestion flow:
[normal] [ New 10-K Document ] --> [ Structural Parser (ToC Tree) ] --> [ Per-Node Granularity Decision ] --> [ Within-Node RAPTOR Sub-Trees ]
[normal] |
[normal] v
[normal] [ User Report Trigger ] <-- [ LangGraph Multi-Agent Engine ] <-- [ Postgres SQL Validation Layer ]
[normal] Structural Parsing: Documents are broken down by headers, preserving layout syntax and embedding markdown tables cleanly alongside their associated prose.
[normal] Hierarchical Indexing: A structural table-of-contents tree is built from the document’s natural headings, and any oversized section is given a within-node RAPTOR sub-tree (summarize-or-split) — the structure-routed hybrid detailed in Module 2.
[normal] Cache Preparation: Long-term vector spaces and local key-value caches are pre-warmed before an analyst starts an interaction.
[Heading 1] 11. Technical Execution Modules
[Heading 2] 11.1 Module 1: Model Inference Optimization (The Muscle)
[normal] To run massive open-weight models (e.g., Llama-3-70B, Mistral-Large, or deep-reasoning variations) on local enterprise servers, Aegis utilizes low-level inference optimization frameworks. Server-side generation is handled by two co-primary engines; edge execution by a third.
[Heading 3] Server-Side Deployment (vLLM & SGLang — co-primary)
[normal] Both engines expose OpenAI-compatible APIs and share the same core throughput mechanisms (continuous batching and paged attention). They are deployed as interchangeable backends; SGLang is preferred for the prefix-heavy and structured-output paths.
[normal] vLLM (baseline concurrent serving): PagedAttention plus continuous batching, broad model coverage, and a mature OpenAI-compatible server. The default engine for general multi-agent concurrency.
[normal] SGLang (preferred for CAG + structured output): Adds RadixAttention, which holds the KV cache of all concurrent requests in a radix tree, enabling multi-level prefix sharing and automatic fork/branch sharing. This matches Aegis’s shape — a shared rulebook prefix and supervisor/system prompts reused across every sub-agent, and forked Bull/Bear scenario branches sharing a parent context. Its constrained-decoding path (XGrammar) is also substantially faster, benefiting the structured JSON audit trails.
[normal] TensorRT-LLM (optional performance overlay): NVIDIA-only compiled engine for maximum throughput/latency once the model and hardware are frozen. Adopted late: it carries significant setup cost (~1–2 weeks) and single-vendor lock-in, so it is not an iteration-phase engine.
[Heading 3] Shared Core Mechanisms (both server engines)
[normal] Continuous Batching (Iteration-Level Scheduling): Instead of standard request-level batching (which waits for all generations in a batch to complete, wasting GPU cycles on short requests), the engine uses token-level execution scheduling. New incoming agent requests are dynamically integrated into the active execution batch at iteration boundaries, significantly driving up concurrency throughput.
[normal] Paged Attention: Mitigates memory bottlenecks caused by the linear growth of the Key-Value (KV) cache. By allocating physical KV cache memory in non-contiguous pages (similar to virtual memory paging in operating systems), Paged Attention sharply reduces KV cache fragmentation, allowing for larger effective context limits and multiple parallel agent operations on a single GPU cluster.
[Heading 3] Engine Interchangeability & Switching
[normal] Because both engines speak the OpenAI-compatible protocol, all LangGraph agents target a single endpoint and remain agnostic to the backend. Switching is therefore a configuration concern, not a code change, escalating as needed:
[normal] Deploy-time config (baseline): An environment/config value selects the live engine. This fis also how the vLLM-vs-SGLang comparison is run in the evaluation suite — flip the config, re-run the regression set, compare.
[normal] Gateway routing (flexible): A thin router (e.g., a LiteLLM-style proxy) routes per workload — prefix-heavy CAG traffic and structured-JSON generation to SGLang, the remainder to vLLM — behind one endpoint.
[normal] Constraint — no shared KV cache: The two engines do not share a KV cache; a prefix warmed on one is not reusable by the other. Routing must therefore be session-sticky (an audit session stays pinned to one engine) to preserve the prefix-cache hits the CAG design depends on.
[Heading 3] Edge / Client Deployment (llama.cpp & GGUF)
[normal] For mobile analysts operating on isolated hardware, models are compiled into GGUF format via llama.cpp.
[normal] Weights undergo 4-bit and 8-bit weight quantization (e.g., Q4_K_M and Q8_0 schemas), dropping local VRAM requirements dramatically while keeping perplexity loss minimal.
[normal] Development tooling note: Ollama (a convenience wrapper around llama.cpp) is used only for local prototyping and model smoke-testing. It is not a concurrent production serving engine and is never placed on the server path, which belongs exclusively to vLLM / SGLang.
[Heading 2] 11.2 Module 2: Advanced & Agentic RAG (The Knowledge)
[normal] Standard semantic search fails on complex financial audits because a single liability risk might be mentioned on page 12 and explained in a footnote on page 240, and because the most-similar chunk is not always the most relevant one. Aegis therefore uses a structure-routed hierarchical retrieval design — a hybrid of PageIndex-style reasoning navigation and RAPTOR-style summarize-or-split — backed by the cache-driven CAG path for static rulebooks.
[Heading 3] Retrieval Design: Structure-Routed Hierarchical Retrieval (PageIndex × RAPTOR)
[normal] Retrieval runs in two phases: structure decides where to look, and a within-node mechanism decides how much to return. This composition cures the characteristic weakness of each technique used alone — RAPTOR’s tendency to sever a fact from the context that explains it, and pure PageIndex’s overflow when a returned section is too large or the answer is a tiny span within it.
[normal] Phase 1 — Structural navigation (PageIndex-style routing). At ingestion, a “table-of-contents” tree is built from the document’s natural headings (e.g., Item 8 → Note 14 (Leases) → sub-notes), each node holding a title and a short summary. At query time an LLM reasons over that compact ToC — not the full text — to navigate to the correct node, following cross-references (“see Note 14”) as a human would. The output is a traceable path to the right section, explainable by construction.
[normal] Phase 2 — Within-node RAPTOR (summarize-or-split). A node does not store one monolithic blob of section text. At ingestion each section is given a granularity decision against a configured size threshold: if it is small enough to use whole, it is stored as-is; if it is oversized, a mini-RAPTOR index is built over just that section’s content (chunk into ~300-token leaves → embed → cluster → summarize into a small local sub-tree). Retrieval is then served by level — a global question about the section returns the node’s compact summary (which always fits), while a local needle question runs a vector search within that node’s leaves only and returns the precise span.
[normal] Global Queries (“What is this firm’s structural exposure to real estate leases?”) are answered from a node’s within-node summary — never by dumping the full section into context.
[normal] Local Queries (“What were the precise Q3 maintenance fees?”) are answered by a span search within the already-correct node’s leaves, pinpointing the sentence rather than returning the whole section.
[Heading 3] Compound Queries: Multi-Node, Mixed-Granularity Retrieval
[normal] A real audit question often spans several sections and needs a different granularity from each — the gist of one section and a pinpointed span from another (e.g., “What is the firm’s overall real-estate strategy, and what was the exact Q3 lease payment?”). The design treats this as the general case, not an exception: “global vs. local” is a property of each retrieved piece, not of the whole query. Navigation therefore decomposes a compound query and emits a set of typed instructions rather than selecting a single node.
[normal] Typed (node, intent) instructions: Navigation decomposes a compound query into sub-questions and returns a list of (node, intent) pairs — each target node tagged with the intent that selected it (global or local). The navigation step carries why each node was chosen, not merely that it was.
[normal] Per-instruction serving (mixed granularity is the norm): FR-RAG-4’s serve rule is applied to each instruction independently — a global-intent node returns its within-node summary; a local-intent node returns a within-node span search. A single answer may therefore combine a summary from one section and a span from another.
[normal] Assembly (budget, dedup, conflict): A composition step assembles the heterogeneous pieces into one context — allocating the token budget across blocks (how many spans per local node, demoting summaries when many sections are touched, when to stop adding nodes), deduplicating overlap, and flagging cross-node conflicts (e.g., a liability stated differently in the lease note vs. the tax note) for the self-correcting loop rather than resolving them silently.
[normal] Richer provenance, not messier: Because each piece carries its own navigation path, a mixed retrieval yields multi-source grounding — a strategy summary from Item 7 (MD&A) alongside a span from Item 8 → Note 14 — which is exactly the multi-source evidence a real audit finding needs.
[Heading 3] RAPTOR (the within-node mechanism)
[normal] Within an oversized node, RAPTOR (Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval) builds the local sub-tree bottom-up: ~300-token sentence-aligned leaves are embedded, soft-clustered using Gaussian Mixture Models (GMMs) (so a chunk relevant to two themes can feed both), and each cluster is summarized by a local LLM into a parent node; the summaries are re-embedded and re-clustered recursively up to a local root. The bottom layer holds fine detail (needle retrieval) and the upper layers hold abstraction (global summaries) — confined to the single section the navigation step selected.
[Heading 3] Why the composition is the chosen design
[normal] Cures severed context: Every leaf lives under its section node, reached by an explicit path, so a retrieved fact always carries its structural context (which section, which note) instead of floating free as in corpus-wide RAPTOR.
[normal] Bounds similarity error: Vector search runs only within an already-correct section — a small, on-topic pool — so “similar-but-wrong” matches are confined to a region the reasoning step already vouched for.
[normal] Keeps the hot path cheap: Navigation reasons only over the compact ToC (titles + summaries), and the expensive per-node sub-trees are built once during async ingestion. One light reasoning call plus a tiny local vector search is far cheaper than reasoning over full sections, preserving the interactive-latency target.
[normal] Native provenance: The navigation path is the audit trail — “Item 8 → Note 14 → [span]” drops directly into the four-tier explainability Grounding tier (Section 12). This is especially strong for the Legal facet, where a clause is navigated to by structure, the precise span is extracted, and the clause’s surrounding section remains intact for meaning.
[Heading 3] Caveats & mitigations
[normal] Navigation latency: The routing LLM call adds latency versus pure vector search; mitigated by keeping the ToC compact, caching navigation for recurring query shapes, and falling back to a within-corpus collapsed-tree vector search when a query is latency-critical.
[normal] Ingestion cost: Building both a ToC tree and per-node sub-trees is more work, but it is asynchronous and therefore fits the decoupled architecture.
[normal] Oversized-node threshold: An explicit, configurable threshold decides when a section gets a sub-tree; it should be calibrated, not hardcoded on intuition.
[normal] Atomic oversized leaves: A genuinely indivisible large unit (e.g., one dense table with no sub-structure) still forces a careful, table-aware split — the single place where the “no arbitrary chunking” ideal must bend.
[Heading 3] Cache-Augmented Generation (CAG) — Staged Implementation
[normal] For high-frequency, unchanging regulatory parameters (e.g., active GAAP accounting rules, IRS tax codes), Aegis avoids per-query vector lookups by keeping the rulebook resident as a precomputed KV state rather than retrieving it at runtime. This is implemented in two stages so that the simplest mechanism is built and measured first, with persistence added only when the data justifies it.
[normal] Tier 1 — Baseline (shared-prefix KV reuse). The regulatory corpus is ordered as a fixed, byte-identical prompt prefix placed ahead of all per-query content, so the engine’s shared-prefix cache reuses its KV blocks across requests — the rulebook is prefilled once and skipped thereafter. This is genuine CAG: no vector search, no re-encoding of the corpus. Both server engines support this mechanism: vLLM via Automatic Prefix Caching (APC), and SGLang via RadixAttention. SGLang is the preferred engine for this path — its radix-tree cache shares the rulebook prefix (and the common supervisor/system prompts) across all concurrent sub-agents and across forked scenario branches, which fits Aegis’s request shape more closely than APC’s per-request hash matching.
[normal] Honest performance characteristic: APC reduces time-to-first-token by eliminating repeated prefill over the rulebook. It is not “zero-latency” — attention is still computed over the cached tokens during decode. The win is the removed prefill, not free inference.
[normal] Honest reliability characteristic: APC is best-effort. Cached blocks are reclaimed under an LRU policy when GPU memory is contended, and the in-process cache does not survive an engine restart (a cold first query re-warms it). Under heavy multi-agent concurrency, the rulebook prefix can therefore be evicted and recomputed.
[normal] Cache isolation (privacy): Because Aegis handles MNPI, prefix-cache reuse is isolated per session. vLLM exposes a per-request cache_salt for this (only requests sharing the same salt may reuse cached blocks, closing the timing side-channel); whichever engine is deployed must enforce equivalent per-session cache isolation.
[normal] Tier 2 — Optional Upgrade (Persistent KV via LMCache). When monitoring shows the rulebook prefix is being evicted and recomputed under real load (see Section 13.1), the KV state is promoted to a persistent tier using LMCache, which provides multi-engine KV storage with tiered GPU→CPU→SSD offload. This guarantees residency across load spikes and process restarts and fits the bounded-hardware, on-prem profile: the rulebook KV lives in CPU/SSD and is loaded into GPU on demand rather than permanently occupying VRAM. Tier 2 is a measured enhancement, not a prerequisite.
[normal] Decision rule: Build Tier 1; instrument the rulebook cache hit rate in the Engineer Control Panel; adopt Tier 2 only when observed eviction/recompute of the regulatory prefix degrades latency. This Tier 1 → Tier 2 transition doubles as a concrete regression-suite exercise (before/after TTFT and hit-rate under induced load).
[Heading 3] Self-Correcting Evaluation Loop
[normal] Every document extraction pass runs inside an autonomous verification envelope using RAGAS metrics programmatically:
[normal] # Programmatic RAGAS Verification Envelope
[normal] faithfulness    = check_faithfulness(retrieved_context, generated_response)
[normal] answer_relevance = check_relevance(user_query, generated_response)
[normal] if faithfulness < 0.85 or answer_relevance < 0.80:
[normal] # Autonomous query reformulation block
[normal] new_query = reformulate_search(user_query, generated_response)
[normal] retry_retrieval_loop(new_query)
[normal] Because retrieval now plans and assembles across nodes, the loop distinguishes three failure classes and routes a different correction to each: a planning failure (decomposition missed a sub-question or mis-assigned its intent) → re-decompose; a navigation failure (routed to the wrong node) → re-navigate; an extraction failure (right node, wrong span or summary) → re-search within the node. Ahead of the faithfulness/relevance gate, a coverage check verifies that the assembled context actually addresses every sub-question the compound query was decomposed into — catching the planning failure that RAGAS faithfulness alone would miss.
[Heading 2] 11.3 Module 3: Advanced Agent Memory Management (The Brain)
[normal] An audit covers weeks of analytical iteration. Standard context windows quickly suffer from token explosion or information erasure. Aegis separates state mechanics into a multi-tiered memory architecture:
[normal] Working Memory: Stores the current task’s active execution-graph state dictionary within individual LangGraph nodes, tracking localized loop conditions.
[normal] Episodic Memory (Short-Term): A rolling context buffer that remembers recent conversational history, analyst adjustments, and intermediate tool responses.
[normal] Semantic Memory (Long-Term): A vector-space database that stores durable, verified analytical facts, past transaction insights, and corporate metrics across separate login sessions.
[normal] Asynchronous Memory Compaction: To keep token overhead minimal, a background compaction loop triggers when episodic memory reaches a set token limit. It processes raw conversational logs, converts them into flat semantic facts, updates the Semantic Vector store, and purges the redundant text transcripts from the active execution path.
[Heading 2] 11.4 Module 4: Multi-Data Source Integration via MCP (The Hands)
[normal] To prevent brittle, hardcoded database endpoints, Aegis uses Anthropic’s open Model Context Protocol (MCP) via standard JSON-RPC protocol engines. This allows agents to interact safely with external tools through a uniform contract.
[Heading 3] PostgreSQL MCP Server
[normal] Exposes secure, parameterized SQL tools to the multi-agent graph. This gives the agent structural system validation access to check corporate text filings against the transactional database.
[normal] /* Outgoing JSON-RPC MCP Tool Invoke Request */
[normal] {
[normal] "jsonrpc": "2.0",
[normal] "method": "tools/call",
[normal] "params": {
[normal] "name": "run_audit_query",
[normal] "arguments": {
[normal] "sql": "SELECT SUM(amount) FROM operating_ledger WHERE account_type = 'lease_deficit'"
[normal] }
[normal] },

[Heading 3] Tableau Dashboards MCP Server
[normal] Allows the agentic network to generate visual assets automatically. The agent updates JSON layout configurations via the Tableau MCP to create or modify risk dashboards, embedding them directly within the web frontend.
[Heading 2] 11.5 Module 5: Hierarchical Agent Framework (The Backbone)
[normal] Aegis handles multi-layered workloads by deploying a Hierarchical Agent State Machine engineered inside LangGraph.
[Heading 3] Node Topography & Routing Strategy
[normal] Supervisor Agent (The Brain): Analyzes the user’s audit goals, initializes the state parameters, coordinates downstream specialists, and runs final synthesis reviews.
[normal] Financial Specialist Agent: Interrogates the structure-routed retrieval index (ToC navigation + within-node RAPTOR) and parses complex balance sheets and table metrics.
[normal] Legal & Compliance Agent: Audits text disclosures against CAG-pinned regulatory rulebooks to identify hidden liabilities.
[normal] Forecasting Agent: Controls forward-looking simulation sandboxes and builds mathematical projection paths.
[normal] Data Tooling Agent: Directly acts as the primary caller for active PostgreSQL and Tableau MCP routing configurations.
[normal] Web Research Agent: Retrieves high-fidelity public web data (latest news, regulatory/market signals) through a citation-preserving search interface, augmenting forecasts while keeping filings/ledger as primary truth (Module 7).
[Heading 3] Observability & Trace Instrumentation
[normal] Every single step — including token generation speeds, tool latencies, node transitions, and self-correction iterations — is piped into a local Arize Phoenix or LangSmith tracing server to ensure complete visibility into the multi-agent graph’s behavior.
