[Heading 3] Node Topography & Routing Strategy
[normal] Supervisor Agent (The Brain): Analyzes the user’s audit goals, initializes the state parameters, coordinates downstream specialists, and runs final synthesis reviews.
[normal] Financial Specialist Agent: Interrogates the structure-routed retrieval index (ToC navigation + within-node RAPTOR) and parses complex balance sheets and table metrics.
[normal] Legal & Compliance Agent: Audits text disclosures against CAG-pinned regulatory rulebooks to identify hidden liabilities.
[normal] Forecasting Agent: Controls forward-looking simulation sandboxes and builds mathematical projection paths.
[normal] Data Tooling Agent: Directly acts as the primary caller for active PostgreSQL and Tableau MCP routing configurations.
[normal] Web Research Agent: Retrieves high-fidelity public web data (latest news, regulatory/market signals) through a citation-preserving search interface, augmenting forecasts while keeping filings/ledger as primary truth (Module 7).
[Heading 3] Observability & Trace Instrumentation
[normal] Every single step — including token generation speeds, tool latencies, node transitions, and self-correction iterations — is piped into a local Arize Phoenix or LangSmith tracing server to ensure complete visibility into the multi-agent graph’s behavior.
[Heading 2] 11.6 Module 6: Model Strategy, Provider Abstraction & Benchmarking
[normal] Aegis runs its own fine-tuned “sovereign” model by default, but treats the model as a pluggable component: any OpenAI-compatible backend — a local engine or an external provider — can be slotted behind the same router used to switch vLLM and SGLang. This yields three benefits: optionality, head-to-head benchmarking of the sovereign model against frontier providers, and the ability to use a frontier provider as a teacher for distillation.
[Heading 3] Operating Modes & Governed Routing
[normal] Sovereign Mode (default): Local-only inference (vLLM / SGLang) with zero external egress. The production mode for any confidential workload.
[normal] External / Benchmarking Mode (opt-in): Routes selected requests to external providers (e.g., OpenAI, Anthropic, Google) through the same OpenAI-compatible router (LiteLLM-style).
[normal] Data-Classification Gate: Every payload carries a sensitivity tag. Only PUBLIC-classified content may leave the host; CONFIDENTIAL/MNPI content is hard-blocked from external routing and the attempt is logged. Because Aegis’s sensitive ledger is simulated and its filings are already public, provider benchmarking and distillation on the public corpus are privacy-safe by construction. This refines, not breaks, Principle P1.
[Heading 3] The Sovereign Model — Base Selection
[normal] Selection criteria: open weights, a permissive license, strong long-context and agentic tool use, solid numerical/table reasoning, and tractable fine-tuning on bounded hardware. Final choice is decided by the benchmarking harness, not asserted up front (Principle P4). Current (2026) candidates:
[normal] Server tier: Qwen3 235B-A22B (Apache-2.0 MoE, broad fine-tune ecosystem); GLM-5.1 (MIT license — a real advantage for commercial fine-tuning, strong agentic/long-horizon reasoning); DeepSeek V4 (best performance-to-cost, ~1M context with a low KV-cache footprint, well suited to retrieval-heavy long-document work).
[normal] Edge / fast-iteration tier: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Apache-2.0 MoE, 35B total / 3B active, ~262K context — strong on bounded hardware); Phi-4 (small, MIT, cheap/fast training iterations — ideal for SFT experimentation); Gemma 4 / Mistral Small 4 (single-GPU and GGUF edge deployment via llama.cpp).
[normal] Recommendation: A two-model family trained on the same data — a larger server model (lead candidates Qwen3-235B or GLM-5.1) and a small edge model (Phi-4 or Qwen3.6-35B-A3B compiled to GGUF).
[Heading 3] Training Strategy (staged, evidence-gated)
[normal] The stages below are the conceptual sequence. QLoRA is the training method used throughout (not a separate stage); SFT and distillation are the objectives. How they are compared, gated, and stopped is specified in the Progressive Training Ladder later in this module.
[normal] Stage 0 — Baseline, no training: Start with a strong instruction-tuned base plus CAG/RAPTOR retrieval and prompting; benchmark against providers. Often retrieval plus a good base suffices — do not train prematurely (P4).
[normal] Stage 1 — Instruction fine-tuning (SFT, LoRA/QLoRA): Adapt to Aegis task formats — faithful 10-K table/footnote extraction, Postgres-MCP SQL generation, and strict four-tier JSON audit trails. Parameter-efficient LoRA/QLoRA keeps training within the hardware budget. Data: curated instruction pairs over real filings + synthetic ledger + regulatory rules mapped to gold audit outputs.
[normal] Stage 2 — Knowledge distillation from a frontier teacher: Use a frontier model (an external provider in External Mode, or a large open model such as DeepSeek V4-Pro / Kimi K2.6) as a teacher to generate high-quality reasoning traces, audit explanations, and tool-use trajectories, then distill the smaller sovereign model on them (response-level and rationale/CoT distillation). This is the second payoff of the provider option — providers serve as teachers, not just benchmarks. Distillation data passes through the governance gate (public/synthetic only).
[normal] Stage 3 — Preference optimization (DPO) for agentic robustness: Pure SFT on static demonstrations compounds errors on multi-step tasks. Following AgentQ, generate preference pairs via guided Monte Carlo Tree Search plus AI self-critique over agent trajectories, then train with an off-policy DPO variant so the agent learns from both successful and sub-optimal paths — improving multi-step planning, tool use, and self-healing. This applies to the audit agents and especially the Web Research agent (Module 7).
[normal] Regression gating: Every checkpoint runs the evaluation/regression suite before promotion (Principle P8).
[Heading 3] Evaluation Methodology (Labeled Task Suite)
[normal] All model decisions — train or not, which provider, which training stage — are made against a fixed, versioned benchmark of representative tasks, each with a known-correct answer. Aegis can build this ground truth cheaply because it already owns the truth: ledger discrepancies are seeded deliberately (the correct delta, account, and risk flag are known), generated SQL can be executed and compared to the true figure, and projection math has a deterministic correct output. The suite spans the system’s capabilities: discrepancy detection, local/global extraction, SQL generation, projection math, and audit-trail formatting.
[normal] Isolate the model from retrieval: Each candidate (sovereign base, provider A/B, trained variants) is fed the identical retrieved context, so only the model varies. Items where all candidates fail on the same context indicate a retrieval problem (fix RAPTOR/CAG/prompting), not a model problem — training cannot fix those.
[normal] Per-capability, gold-label metrics: Discrepancy recall (the headline), SQL execution accuracy, projection numeric exactness, audit-trail completeness (a schema check on the four tiers), and RAGAS faithfulness for open-ended extraction — plus latency and dollar-cost per task. Where an LLM-as-judge is unavoidable, a provider never grades its own output; gold labels or a neutral judge are used to avoid bias.
[normal] Reproducibility: Evaluation runs at temperature 0 so deltas reflect capability, not sampling noise; gaps are confirmed with a paired significance test across items before they count.
[Heading 3] Benchmarking Against External Providers
[normal] Because local and external backends share one OpenAI-compatible interface, the same suite runs unchanged against the sovereign model and each external provider. The provider score is not a target to switch to — it is the rung marker that quantifies how far the local model is from the frontier and, when training is warranted, supplies the distillation teacher. The destination is always Sovereign mode; the provider is the measuring stick and the teacher, not the deployment.
[Heading 3] Train / Don’t-Train Decision Rule
[normal] “Underperforms” is only actionable once made precise. The decision is two ordered questions, not “is base worse than the provider”:
[normal] Does base + retrieval clear the absolute bar the task requires? Bars are set from the use case, not the provider (illustrative defaults, to be calibrated: discrepancy recall ≥ 95%, SQL execution accuracy ≥ 98%, faithfulness ≥ 0.85, audit-trail completeness = 100%). If base clears the bar, ship base and stop — even if a provider scores higher. Staying local is itself a value (P1); the application needs “good enough,” not “better.”
[normal] If base fails the bar, is the gap real and model-shaped? Real = statistically significant across enough items, not run-to-run variance. Model-shaped = the provider cleared it on the same retrieved context, confirming capability rather than retrieval.
[normal] This yields a four-way decision:
[normal] Judge per capability, not globally: base may clear the bar for extraction yet fail SQL generation, in which case only the SQL skill is trained — cheaper and lower-risk than retraining everything.
[Heading 3] Progressive Training Ladder
[normal] When training is warranted, it runs as a measured staircase, each rung benchmarked against the provider baseline so the gain of each step is attributable. A category distinction is kept explicit: QLoRA is the training method (held constant — it fits training on bounded hardware by freezing a quantized base and training small low-rank adapters); SFT and knowledge distillation are training objectives run through that method. They differ only in where the labels come from — SFT uses Aegis’s own gold targets; distillation uses the teacher provider’s outputs and reasoning traces.
[normal] Three objective configurations, all via QLoRA: SFT-only, distillation-only, and SFT-then-distillation — each forked from the same base. The combined run is the likely production recipe; the two isolated runs are diagnostic, revealing whether the stages are additive, redundant, or in conflict.
[normal] Merge between stages: For SFT-then-distillation, the SFT adapter is merged into the base before the distillation pass, so each rung is a clean frozen starting point and adapter interactions do not confound the measurement.
[normal] Watch a per-capability curve, not one number: Training often trades one skill for another (e.g., improving SQL while degrading faithfulness). Each rung is gated by the Optimization Regression Engine (Section 13.1): no previously-passing capability may regress.
[normal] Stopping rule: Climb only until a rung clears every capability bar, then ship that rung — the lowest-complexity variant that passes — even if the provider is still nominally ahead. If SFT-only clears the bar, distillation is never paid for.
[normal] Diagnostic payoff: The gap closed at each rung is itself informative: if SFT closes most of it, the problem was format/behaviour; if only distillation closes it, the problem is genuine reasoning capability — which may instead argue for a larger base model rather than more distillation.
[Heading 2] 11.7 Module 7: Web Research & Real-Time Grounding Agent
[normal] Forecasts must reflect current reality — latest news, tariff and regulatory announcements, market conditions — not only the filings. Aegis adds a Web Research agent (a LangGraph specialist) that retrieves high-fidelity public web data and folds it into projections, while the SEC filings and ledger remain the primary source of truth.
[Heading 3] Retrieval Layer (agent-native search, not raw SERP)
[normal] The agent uses AI-native search APIs that return clean, structured, cited content with source URLs and timestamps (for the audit trail), rather than human-oriented result snippets that require a separate scraping layer.
[normal] Candidates (2026): Exa (semantic search with company/finance/news categories, field-level citations, and a zero-data-retention option); Tavily (agent-native, with finance and news topics, recency filters, and a multi-step research endpoint); Firecrawl (clean markdown plus full metadata/timestamps, strong deep extraction); Valyu (covers SEC filings and finance sources, strong on finance and time-sensitive benchmarks); Perplexity Sonar / Brave for synthesized or independent-index needs.
[normal] Recommendation: A provider-agnostic search abstraction (mirroring the model abstraction), integrated via MCP — these providers publish MCP servers, consistent with P7 — defaulting to a finance-capable, citation-preserving, ZDR provider (Exa or Valyu) with Firecrawl for deep page extraction. Prefer primary sources (SEC, company investor relations, reputable financial press).
[Heading 3] Autonomous Research Loop (AgentQ-inspired)
[normal] For multi-step research the agent runs an iterative deep-research loop: decompose the objective, search, read/extract, update its working knowledge, re-query with refined questions, and stop at a coverage or budget limit — the documented shift from one-shot search to agentic research. It borrows AgentQ’s robustness mechanisms: guided search (MCTS-style exploration balancing exploration and exploitation), AI self-critique at each step as an intermediate reward to steer the search and catch dead ends, and — when training — DPO over successful versus failed research trajectories to make the agent self-healing and resistant to compounding errors.
[Heading 3] Grounding & Provenance Discipline (non-negotiable)
[normal] Augmentation, not authority: Web data adds timely context (e.g., a just-announced tariff that sharpens a bear case); the crux of every forecast derives from the filings and ledger the system already holds.
[normal] Provenance: Every web-sourced claim carries its source URL and retrieval timestamp into the Grounding tier of the four-tier audit trail, tagged distinctly from filing-sourced claims so reviewers can separate internal from external evidence.
[normal] Conflict handling: Disagreements between web data and filings trigger the RAGAS self-correcting loop and are surfaced, never silently resolved.
[normal] Quality controls: Recency/time-range filters, source reputation and whitelisting, deduplication, and faithfulness scoring of web-augmented answers.
[Heading 3] Privacy & Governance
[normal] Web queries are built from public entity names and public facts only; MNPI and ledger specifics are never placed in a query or URL. ZDR search providers are preferred, and the agent is subject to the same data-classification gate as Module 6.
[Heading 1] 12. Projections, Scenario Analysis, & Hyper-Granular Explainability
[normal] Aegis goes beyond traditional search paradigms by allowing analysts to run predictive “What-If” calculations (e.g., “Simulate a 15% increase in operational supply costs due to the supply chain tariffs flagged on page 45 of the 10-K”). Scenarios may incorporate real-time signals supplied by the Web Research agent (Module 7) — provenance-tagged and treated as augmentation — while the filings and ledger remain the primary basis for every projection.
[Heading 2] 12.1 Deterministic Arithmetic Sandboxing
[normal] LLMs are notoriously unreliable for multi-step math calculations. To ensure complete numerical accuracy, the Forecasting Agent never attempts complex math inline. Instead, it extracts the target metrics via the Postgres MCP, writes a clean Python calculation script, and executes it inside an isolated code sandbox. The calculated data matrices are then parsed back into the agentic workflow.
[Heading 2] 12.2 The Mathematical Audit Trail
[normal] To ensure boardroom trust, any projection or discovered deficit must produce an explicit, granular explanation trail.
[normal] Let B represent the historical financial baseline pulled from the PostgreSQL ledger, δ represent the user’s parameter variance modifier, and L_f define the projected future operational liability. The agent processes calculations using a deterministic sequence:
[normal] L_f = B * (1 + δ)^t
[normal] The generated answer is displayed in the UI using a Four-Tier Verification Layout:
[normal] The Premise: The baseline value extracted from the Postgres ledger (including transaction log references).
[normal] The Parameterization: The specific user input delta applied to the calculation matrix.
[normal] The Equation: The explicit formula executed within the calculation sandbox.
[normal] The Grounding: The exact section text and page quote extracted from the RAPTOR index, explaining why that specific risk is compounding.
[normal] +----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
[normal] |                          EXPLAINABILITY AUDIT TRAIL                        |
[normal] +----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
[normal] | [1] LEDGER BASELINE (Postgres MCP): $142,500,000.00 (ID: L-98421)          |
[normal] | [2] USER VARIANCE PARAMETER       : Delta +15.00% Operational Cost Surge   |
[normal] | [3] APPLIED CALCULATED MATRIX     : $142,500,000 * (1 + 0.15)^1 = $163,875,000 |
[normal] | [4] GROUNDED SOURCE TEXT (RAPTOR) : 'Section 1A, Risk Factors, p. 45:      |
[normal] |     Tariff re-adjustments on raw materials projected to drive base +12-18%'|
[normal] +----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
[Heading 2] 12.3 Memory Compartmentalization
[normal] When running multiple scenario models simultaneously (e.g., Bull Case vs. Bear Case), the Supervisor Agent instantiates completely separate LangGraph thread contexts. This keeps the data streams isolated and prevents numbers from Scenario A from leaking into the context window of Scenario B.
[Heading 1] 13. Evaluation, Drift Suite, & User Interface
[normal] To move this system beyond an experimental phase and turn it into a true AI platform engineering piece, Aegis features a production-grade testing and optimization workspace.
[Heading 2] 13.1 The Continuous Evaluation Suite
[normal] The system includes a dedicated engineering panel running automated continuous assessment engines:
[normal] Continuous RAGAS Logging: Tracks Faithfulness, Context Recall, and Answer Relevance scores over all active sessions to capture long-term performance trends.
[normal] Data & Concept Drift Monitors: Monitors the distribution characteristics of incoming document formats and database transaction volumes. If target firms shift their reporting styles or tax codes change, the monitor triggers a “Concept Drift Warning,” indicating that the RAPTOR index tree needs to be rebuilt.
[normal] Optimization Regression Engine: Whenever a system administrator changes optimization layers (e.g., swapping a 4-bit model in llama.cpp for an 8-bit weights-only configuration on a vLLM node, or switching the server engine between vLLM and SGLang), the system initiates a background testing script. This engine runs synthetic multi-step prompt evaluation sets to verify that the optimized layout has not caused a regression in the model’s analytical reasoning or mathematical accuracy.
[normal] KV Cache Residency Monitor (new in v2.0): Tracks the hit rate and eviction frequency of the CAG regulatory prefix (Module 2, Tier 1). A sustained drop in hit rate under concurrent load is the explicit trigger for promoting the rulebook to the Tier 2 persistent cache (LMCache). Before/after time-to-first-token is captured as a regression-suite artifact.
[Heading 2] 13.2 User Interface Architecture
[normal] The front-end layout is cleanly divided into two separate workspaces:
[normal] The Financial Analyst Workspace: A streamlined interface containing document drop nodes, conversational panels, report configuration triggers, and interactive Tableau charts showing projected operational deficits.
[normal] The Platform Engineer Control Panel: Provides deep system observability. It monitors vLLM / SGLang iteration batching speeds, token-per-second generation metrics, active memory allocations across the multi-tiered memory caches, RAGAS health indices, KV cache residency / hit-rate (including RadixAttention prefix reuse), and system drift parameters.
[Heading 1] 14. Implementation Roadmap
[normal] To begin building Project Aegis step-by-step, follow a structured execution path:
[normal] Phase 1 (Data & Storage Setup): Write the initial script to download target 10-K filings using programmatic SEC tools and build the Python Faker template to populate the matching local PostgreSQL ledger.
[normal] Phase 2 (Inference Architecture): Stand up the co-primary server engines (vLLM and SGLang) behind a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint, enable shared-prefix KV reuse (APC / RadixAttention) with per-session cache isolation, configure llama.cpp / GGUF for edge clients, and verify token delivery metrics. Ollama is used for local dev only.
[normal] Phase 3 (Ingestion & RAG): Build the structure-routed hybrid index (PageIndex-style ToC navigation + within-node RAPTOR summarize-or-split with an oversized-node threshold), construct the self-correcting RAG loop, and establish the CAG rulebook prefix (Tier 1).
[normal] Phase 4 (Agentic Routing): Define the multi-agent LangGraph node relationships and implement the Model Context Protocol (MCP) integrations.
[normal] Phase 5 (Evaluation & Hardening): Stand up the RAGAS logging, drift monitors, regression engine, and KV residency monitor; evaluate whether the Tier 2 persistent cache is warranted.
[normal] Phase 6 (Model Strategy & Benchmarking): Build the provider abstraction and data-classification gate; baseline the sovereign base model against external providers; then run staged training (SFT → distillation → DPO), each stage regression-gated, and pick the production model from the benchmark.
[normal] Phase 7 (Web Research Agent): Add the provider-agnostic web search abstraction via MCP, implement the AgentQ-style autonomous research loop, and wire web provenance and conflict handling into the audit trail and self-correcting loop.
[normal] Phase 8 (Holistic Data Expansion): Light up the remaining facets in order of cost — Operational (extend the Postgres schema with customer/supplier/HR tables), then Legal (onboard a contract corpus via RAPTOR using EDGAR exhibits / CUAD / a simulated data room), then Compliance (add the compliance register paired with CAG rules). Each facet ships behind the data-adaptive contract (Section 10.2), with coverage reporting, before the next is added.
[Heading 1] 15. Open Items & Risk Register
[normal] The following technical claims from the original draft were left unchanged in this revision but should be pressure-tested before implementation:
[normal] Paged Attention fragmentation figure: The original draft cited a reduction from ~60% fragmentation to “near 0%.” The qualitative claim (large reduction) is sound; the specific figures should be replaced with measured numbers from your own hardware rather than stated as fact.
[normal] RAGAS threshold values: The faithfulness < 0.85 and answer_relevance < 0.80 cutoffs are placeholders. These should be calibrated against a labeled validation set, not hardcoded on intuition.
[normal] RAPTOR leaf size (~300 tokens): A reasonable default, but optimal chunk size for dense financial tables vs. prose differs; worth an ablation.
[Heading 1] 16. Glossary
[normal] RAPTOR: Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval — a hierarchical index built by recursively clustering and summarizing text into a tree; used here as the within-node summarize-or-split mechanism.
[normal] PageIndex: A vectorless, reasoning-based retrieval approach that builds a table-of-contents tree from a document’s natural structure and uses LLM reasoning to navigate to relevant sections; used here for the structural-navigation phase.
[normal] Structure-Routed Hybrid Retrieval: Aegis’s retrieval design — PageIndex-style ToC navigation to the correct node, then within-node RAPTOR (summary for global queries, span search for local queries) to avoid context overflow and needle-in-section loss.
[normal] Retrieval Plan / (node, intent) instruction: The set of typed instructions navigation emits for a compound query, each pairing a target node with the granularity its sub-question needs (global summary or local span).
[normal] Coverage Check: A verification, ahead of the faithfulness gate, that the assembled retrieved context addresses every sub-question a compound query was decomposed into.
[normal] CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation): Serving a fixed corpus by keeping its precomputed KV state resident and reused, instead of retrieving it per query.
[normal] KV Cache: The Key/Value vectors computed per token during prefill; caching them avoids recomputation on reuse.
[normal] APC (Automatic Prefix Caching): vLLM’s best-effort reuse of KV blocks across requests that share an exact prompt prefix; LRU-evicted.
[normal] RadixAttention: SGLang’s prefix-cache mechanism storing KV state in a radix tree, enabling multi-level prefix sharing and fork/branch sharing across all concurrent requests.
[normal] PagedAttention: vLLM technique that stores the KV cache in non-contiguous fixed-size pages to eliminate memory fragmentation.
[normal] Continuous Batching: Iteration-level scheduling that admits new requests into the running batch at token boundaries to raise throughput.
[normal] TTFT: Time-to-first-token — latency from request to the first generated token; reduced by prefix caching.
[normal] GGUF: The quantized model file format used by llama.cpp for edge/CPU deployment.
[normal] Quantization (Q4_K_M / Q8_0): Reducing weight precision to 4-/8-bit to cut memory use with minimal quality loss.
[normal] MCP (Model Context Protocol): Anthropic’s open JSON-RPC protocol letting agents call external tools (e.g., Postgres, Tableau) through a uniform contract.
[normal] RAGAS: A framework of metrics (e.g., faithfulness, answer relevance, context recall) used to score and gate RAG output.
[normal] GMM (Gaussian Mixture Model): The clustering method RAPTOR uses to group leaf embeddings before summarization.
[normal] MNPI: Material Non-Public Information — confidential data whose external transmission is prohibited.
[normal] LMCache: A persistent, tiered (GPU→CPU→SSD) KV-cache store used for CAG Tier 2 residency guarantees.
[normal] Working / Episodic / Semantic Memory: Active task state / short-term session buffer / long-term durable fact store, respectively.
[normal] Memory Compaction: A background process that distills episodic logs into semantic facts and purges redundant transcripts.
[normal] Concept / Data Drift: Shifts in document structure, reporting style, or data distribution that may require index rebuilds.
[normal] Self-Correcting Loop: A generation cycle wrapped in RAGAS scoring that reformulates and retries when quality is too low.
[normal] Supervisor / Specialist Agent: The orchestrating LangGraph node and its delegated domain nodes (Financial, Legal, Forecasting, Data-Tooling).
[normal] LangGraph: The framework used to express the hierarchical agent state machine.
[normal] Vector DB (Qdrant): The store holding embeddings for RAPTOR leaves and semantic memory.
[normal] EDGAR: The SEC’s public system for downloading company filings (10-K / 10-Q).
[normal] CDC (Change Data Capture): Detecting new/changed records (files or ledger rows) to trigger event-driven ingestion.
[normal] Claims layer vs. Evidence layer: What a filing asserts (claims) versus the granular records that verify it (evidence); auditing is the cross-check of one against the other.
[normal] Capability Tiers / Data-Adaptive Operation: The model by which Aegis runs on a minimal data set and progressively enables more diligence facets (Legal, Operational, Compliance) as their evidence layers are added.
[normal] Data Room: The secured repository where, in a real transaction, a target company lawfully provides its internal records (including MNPI) to the diligence party under NDA.
[normal] CUAD: The Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset — a public, clause-annotated corpus of legal contracts usable as a real proxy for the Legal facet.
[normal] Sovereign Model: Aegis’s own fine-tuned, locally-hosted model — the default inference backend.
[normal] Provider Abstraction / Operating Modes: A router that makes local engines and external providers interchangeable; Sovereign Mode is local-only, External Mode is governed opt-in.
[normal] Data-Classification Gate: Policy layer that blocks confidential/MNPI payloads from external routing, permitting only public/synthetic data to leave the host.
[normal] SFT (Supervised / Instruction Fine-Tuning): Training a base model on curated instruction–response pairs to adapt it to task formats.
[normal] LoRA / QLoRA: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that train small adapter weights (QLoRA over a quantized base), enabling training on bounded hardware.
[normal] Knowledge Distillation (Teacher/Student): Training a smaller student model on the outputs or reasoning traces of a larger teacher model.
[normal] DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): Preference-based training on pairs of better/worse responses; used here for multi-step agentic r

23	[normal] Server tier: Qwen3 235B-A22B (Apache-2.0 MoE, broad fine-tune ecosystem); GLM-5.1 (MIT license — a real advantage for commercial fine-tuning, strong agentic/long-horizon reasoning); DeepSeek V4 (best performance-to-cost, ~1M context with a low KV-cache footprint, well suited to retrieval-heavy long-document work).
24	[normal] Edge / fast-iteration tier: Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Apache-2.0 MoE, 35B total / 3B active, ~262K context — strong on bounded hardware); Phi-4 (small, MIT, cheap/fast training iterations — ideal for SFT experimentation); Gemma 4 / Mistral Small 4 (single-GPU and GGUF edge deployment via llama.cpp).
25	[normal] Recommendation: A two-model family trained on the same data — a larger server model (lead candidates Qwen3-235B or GLM-5.1) and a small edge model (Phi-4 or Qwen3.6-35B-A3B compiled to GGUF).
26	[Heading 3] Training Strategy (staged, evidence-gated)
27	[normal] The stages below are the conceptual sequence. QLoRA is the training method used throughout (not a separate stage); SFT and distillation are the objectives. How they are compared, gated, and stopped is specified in the Progressive Training Ladder later in this module.
28	[normal] Stage 0 — Baseline, no training: Start with a strong instruction-tuned base plus CAG/RAPTOR retrieval and prompting; benchmark against providers. Often retrieval plus a good base suffices — do not train prematurely (P4).
29	[normal] Stage 1 — Instruction fine-tuning (SFT, LoRA/QLoRA): Adapt to Aegis task formats — faithful 10-K table/footnote extraction, Postgres-MCP SQL generation, and strict four-tier JSON audit trails. Parameter-efficient LoRA/QLoRA keeps training within the hardware budget. Data: curated instruction pairs over real filings + synthetic ledger + regulatory rules mapped to gold audit outputs.
30	[normal] Stage 2 — Knowledge distillation from a frontier teacher: Use a frontier model (an external provider in External Mode, or a large open model such as DeepSeek V4-Pro / Kimi K2.6) as a teacher to generate high-quality reasoning traces, audit explanations, and tool-use trajectories, then distill the smaller sovereign model on them (response-level and rationale/CoT distillation). This is the second payoff of the provider option — providers serve as teachers, not just benchmarks. Distillation data passes through the governance gate (public/synthetic only).
31	[normal] Stage 3 — Preference optimization (DPO) for agentic robustness: Pure SFT on static demonstrations compounds errors on multi-step tasks. Following AgentQ, generate preference pairs via guided Monte Carlo Tree Search plus AI self-critique over agent trajectories, then train with an off-policy DPO variant so the agent learns from both successful and sub-optimal paths — improving multi-step planning, tool use, and self-healing. This applies to the audit agents and especially the Web Research agent (Module 7).
32	[normal] Regression gating: Every checkpoint runs the evaluation/regression suite before promotion (Principle P8).
33	[Heading 3] Evaluation Methodology (Labeled Task Suite)
34	[normal] All model decisions — train or not, which provider, which training stage — are made against a fixed, versioned benchmark of representative tasks, each with a known-correct answer. Aegis can build this ground truth cheaply because it already owns the truth: ledger discrepancies are seeded deliberately (the correct delta, account, and risk flag are known), generated SQL can be executed and compared to the true figure, and projection math has a deterministic correct output. The suite spans the system’s capabilities: discrepancy detection, local/global extraction, SQL generation, projection math, and audit-trail formatting.
35	[normal] Isolate the model from retrieval: Each candidate (sovereign base, provider A/B, trained variants) is fed the identical retrieved context, so only the model varies. Items where all candidates fail on the same context indicate a retrieval problem (fix RAPTOR/CAG/prompting), not a model problem — training cannot fix those.
36	[normal] Per-capability, gold-label metrics: Discrepancy recall (the headline), SQL execution accuracy, projection numeric exactness, audit-trail completeness (a schema check on the four tiers), and RAGAS faithfulness for open-ended extraction — plus latency and dollar-cost per task. Where an LLM-as-judge is unavoidable, a provider never grades its own output; gold labels or a neutral judge are used to avoid bias.
37	[normal] Reproducibility: Evaluation runs at temperature 0 so deltas reflect capability, not sampling noise; gaps are confirmed with a paired significance test across items before they count.
38	[Heading 3] Benchmarking Against External Providers
39	[normal] Because local and external backends share one OpenAI-compatible interface, the same suite runs unchanged against the sovereign model and each external provider. The provider score is not a target to switch to — it is the rung marker that quantifies how far the local model is from the frontier and, when training is warranted, supplies the distillation teacher. The destination is always Sovereign mode; the provider is the measuring stick and the teacher, not the deployment.
40	[Heading 3] Train / Don’t-Train Decision Rule
41	[normal] “Underperforms” is only actionable once made precise. The decision is two ordered questions, not “is base worse than the provider”:
42	[normal] Does base + retrieval clear the absolute bar the task requires? Bars are set from the use case, not the provider (illustrative defaults, to be calibrated: discrepancy recall ≥ 95%, SQL execution accuracy ≥ 98%, faithfulness ≥ 0.85, audit-trail completeness = 100%). If base clears the bar, ship base and stop — even if a provider scores higher. Staying local is itself a value (P1); the application needs “good enough,” not “better.”
43	[normal] If base fails the bar, is the gap real and model-shaped? Real = statistically significant across enough items, not run-to-run variance. Model-shaped = the provider cleared it on the same retrieved context, confirming capability rather than retrieval.
44	[normal] This yields a four-way decision:
45	[normal] Judge per capability, not globally: base may clear the bar for extraction yet fail SQL generation, in which case only the SQL skill is trained — cheaper and lower-risk than retraining everything.
46	[Heading 3] Progressive Training Ladder
47	[normal] When training is warranted, it runs as a measured staircase, each rung benchmarked against the provider baseline so the gain of each step is attributable. A category distinction is kept explicit: QLoRA is the training method (held constant — it fits training on bounded hardware by freezing a quantized base and training small low-rank adapters); SFT and knowledge distillation are training objectives run through that method. They differ only in where the labels come from — SFT uses Aegis’s own gold targets; distillation uses the teacher provider’s outputs and reasoning traces.
48	[normal] Three objective configurations, all via QLoRA: SFT-only, distillation-only, and SFT-then-distillation — each forked from the same base. The combined run is the likely production recipe; the two isolated runs are diagnostic, revealing whether the stages are additive, redundant, or in conflict.
49	[normal] Merge between stages: For SFT-then-distillation, the SFT adapter is merged into the base before the distillation pass, so each rung is a clean frozen starting point and adapter interactions do not confound the measurement.
50	[normal] Watch a per-capability curve, not one number: Training often trades one skill for another (e.g., improving SQL while degrading faithfulness). Each rung is gated by the Optimization Regression Engine (Section 13.1): no previously-passing capability may regress.
51	[normal] Stopping rule: Climb only until a rung clears every capability bar, then ship that rung — the lowest-complexity variant that passes — even if the provider is still nominally ahead. If SFT-only clears the bar, distillation is never paid for.
52	[normal] Diagnostic payoff: The gap closed at each rung is itself informative: if SFT closes most of it, the problem was format/behaviour; if only distillation closes it, the problem is genuine reasoning capability — which may instead argue for a larger base model rather than more distillation.
53	[Heading 2] 11.7 Module 7: Web Research & Real-Time Grounding Agent
54	[normal] Forecasts must reflect current reality — latest news, tariff and regulatory announcements, market conditions — not only the filings. Aegis adds a Web Research agent (a LangGraph specialist) that retrieves high-fidelity public web data and folds it into projections, while the SEC filings and ledger remain the primary source of truth.
55	[Heading 3] Retrieval Layer (agent-native search, not raw SERP)
56	[normal] The agent uses AI-native search APIs that return clean, structured, cited content with source URLs and timestamps (for the audit trail), rather than human-oriented result snippets that require a separate scraping layer.
57	[normal] Candidates (2026): Exa (semantic search with company/finance/news categories, field-level citations, and a zero-data-retention option); Tavily (agent-native, with finance and news topics, recency filters, and a multi-step research endpoint); Firecrawl (clean markdown plus full metadata/timestamps, strong deep extraction); Valyu (covers SEC filings and finance sources, strong on finance and time-sensitive benchmarks); Perplexity Sonar / Brave for synthesized or independent-index needs.
58	[normal] Recommendation: A provider-agnostic search abstraction (mirroring the model abstraction), integrated via MCP — these providers publish MCP servers, consistent with P7 — defaulting to a finance-capable, citation-preserving, ZDR provider (Exa or Valyu) with Firecrawl for deep page extraction. Prefer primary sources (SEC, company investor relations, reputable financial press).
59	[Heading 3] Autonomous Research Loop (AgentQ-inspired)
60	[normal] For multi-step research the agent runs an iterative deep-research loop: decompose the objective, search, read/extract, update its working knowledge, re-query with refined questions, and stop at a coverage or budget limit — the documented shift from one-shot search to agentic research. It borrows AgentQ’s robustness mechanisms: guided search (MCTS-style exploration balancing exploration and exploitation), AI self-critique at each step as an intermediate reward to steer the search and catch dead ends, and — when training — DPO over successful versus failed research trajectories to make the agent self-healing and resistant to compounding errors.
61	[Heading 3] Grounding & Provenance Discipline (non-negotiable)
62	[normal] Augmentation, not authority: Web data adds timely context (e.g., a just-announced tariff that sharpens a bear case); the crux of every forecast derives from the filings and ledger the system already holds.
63	[normal] Provenance: Every web-sourced claim carries its source URL and retrieval timestamp into the Grounding tier of the four-tier audit trail, tagged distinctly from filing-sourced claims so reviewers can separate internal from external evidence.
64	[normal] Conflict handling: Disagreements between web data and filings trigger the RAGAS self-correcting loop and are surfaced, never silently resolved.
65	[normal] Quality controls: Recency/time-range filters, source reputation and whitelisting, deduplication, and faithfulness scoring of web-augmented answers.
66	[Heading 3] Privacy & Governance
67	[normal] Web queries are built from public entity names and public facts only; MNPI and ledger specifics are never placed in a query or URL. ZDR search providers are preferred, and the agent is subject to the same data-classification gate as Module 6.
68	[Heading 1] 12. Projections, Scenario Analysis, & Hyper-Granular Explainability
69	[normal] Aegis goes beyond traditional search paradigms by allowing analysts to run predictive “What-If” calculations (e.g., “Simulate a 15% increase in operational supply costs due to the supply chain tariffs flagged on page 45 of the 10-K”). Scenarios may incorporate real-time signals supplied by the Web Research agent (Module 7) — provenance-tagged and treated as augmentation — while the filings and ledger remain the primary basis for every projection.
70	[Heading 2] 12.1 Deterministic Arithmetic Sandboxing
71	[normal] LLMs are notoriously unreliable for multi-step math calculations. To ensure complete numerical accuracy, the Forecasting Agent never attempts complex math inline. Instead, it extracts the target metrics via the Postgres MCP, writes a clean Python calculation script, and executes it inside an isolated code sandbox. The calculated data matrices are then parsed back into the agentic workflow.
72	[Heading 2] 12.2 The Mathematical Audit Trail
73	[normal] To ensure boardroom trust, any projection or discovered deficit must produce an explicit, granular explanation trail.
74	[normal] Let B represent the historical financial baseline pulled from the PostgreSQL ledger, δ represent the user’s parameter variance modifier, and L_f define the projected future operational liability. The agent processes calculations using a deterministic sequence:
75	[normal] L_f = B * (1 + δ)^t
76	[normal] The generated answer is displayed in the UI using a Four-Tier Verification Layout:
77	[normal] The Premise: The baseline value extracted from the Postgres ledger (including transaction log references).
78	[normal] The Parameterization: The specific user input delta applied to the calculation matrix.
79	[normal] The Equation: The explicit formula executed within the calculation sandbox.
80	[normal] The Grounding: The exact section text and page quote extracted from the RAPTOR index, explaining why that specific risk is compounding.
81	[normal] +----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
82	[normal] |                          EXPLAINABILITY AUDIT TRAIL                        |
83	[normal] +----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
84	[normal] | [1] LEDGER BASELINE (Postgres MCP): $142,500,000.00 (ID: L-98421)          |
85	[normal] | [2] USER VARIANCE PARAMETER       : Delta +15.00% Operational Cost Surge   |
86	[normal] | [3] APPLIED CALCULATED MATRIX     : $142,500,000 * (1 + 0.15)^1 = $163,875,000 |
87	[normal] | [4] GROUNDED SOURCE TEXT (RAPTOR) : 'Section 1A, Risk Factors, p. 45:      |
88	[normal] |     Tariff re-adjustments on raw materials projected to drive base +12-18%'|
89	[normal] +----------------------------------------------------------------------------+
90	[Heading 2] 12.3 Memory Compartmentalization
91	[normal] When running multiple scenario models simultaneously (e.g., Bull Case vs. Bear Case), the Supervisor Agent instantiates completely separate LangGraph thread contexts. This keeps the data streams isolated and prevents numbers from Scenario A from leaking into the context window of Scenario B.
92	[Heading 1] 13. Evaluation, Drift Suite, & User Interface
93	[normal] To move this system beyond an experimental phase and turn it into a true AI platform engineering piece, Aegis features a production-grade testing and optimization workspace.
94	[Heading 2] 13.1 The Continuous Evaluation Suite
95	[normal] The system includes a dedicated engineering panel running automated continuous assessment engines:
96	[normal] Continuous RAGAS Logging: Tracks Faithfulness, Context Recall, and Answer Relevance scores over all active sessions to capture long-term performance trends.
97	[normal] Data & Concept Drift Monitors: Monitors the distribution characteristics of incoming document formats and database transaction volumes. If target firms shift their reporting styles or tax codes change, the monitor triggers a “Concept Drift Warning,” indicating that the RAPTOR index tree needs to be rebuilt.
98	[normal] Optimization Regression Engine: Whenever a system administrator changes optimization layers (e.g., swapping a 4-bit model in llama.cpp for an 8-bit weights-only configuration on a vLLM node, or switching the server engine between vLLM and SGLang), the system initiates a background testing script. This engine runs synthetic multi-step prompt evaluation sets to verify that the optimized layout has not caused a regression in the model’s analytical reasoning or mathematical accuracy.
99	[normal] KV Cache Residency Monitor (new in v2.0): Tracks the hit rate and eviction frequency of the CAG regulatory prefix (Module 2, Tier 1). A sustained drop in hit rate under concurrent load is the explicit trigger for promoting the rulebook to the Tier 2 persistent cache (LMCache). Before/after time-to-first-token is captured as a regression-suite artifact.
100	[Heading 2] 13.2 User Interface Architecture
101	[normal] The front-end layout is cleanly divided into two separate workspaces:
102	[normal] The Financial Analyst Workspace: A streamlined interface containing document drop nodes, conversational panels, report configuration triggers, and interactive Tableau charts showing projected operational deficits.
103	[normal] The Platform Engineer Control Panel: Provides deep system observability. It monitors vLLM / SGLang iteration batching speeds, token-per-second generation metrics, active memory allocations across the multi-tiered memory caches, RAGAS health indices, KV cache residency / hit-rate (including RadixAttention prefix reuse), and system drift parameters.
104	[Heading 1] 14. Implementation Roadmap
105	[normal] To begin building Project Aegis step-by-step, follow a structured execution path:
106	[normal] Phase 1 (Data & Storage Setup): Write the initial script to download target 10-K filings using programmatic SEC tools and build the Python Faker template to populate the matching local PostgreSQL ledger.
107	[normal] Phase 2 (Inference Architecture): Stand up the co-primary server engines (vLLM and SGLang) behind a single OpenAI-compatible endpoint, enable shared-prefix KV reuse (APC / RadixAttention) with per-session cache isolation, configure llama.cpp / GGUF for edge clients, and verify token delivery metrics. Ollama is used for local dev only.
108	[normal] Phase 3 (Ingestion & RAG): Build the structure-routed hybrid index (PageIndex-style ToC navigation + within-node RAPTOR summarize-or-split with an oversized-node threshold), construct the self-correcting RAG loop, and establish the CAG rulebook prefix (Tier 1).
109	[normal] Phase 4 (Agentic Routing): Define the multi-agent LangGraph node relationships and implement the Model Context Protocol (MCP) integrations.
110	[normal] Phase 5 (Evaluation & Hardening): Stand up the RAGAS logging, drift monitors, regression engine, and KV residency monitor; evaluate whether the Tier 2 persistent cache is warranted.
111	[normal] Phase 6 (Model Strategy & Benchmarking): Build the provider abstraction and data-classification gate; baseline the sovereign base model against external providers; then run staged training (SFT → distillation → DPO), each stage regression-gated, and pick the production model from the benchmark.
112	[normal] Phase 7 (Web Research Agent): Add the provider-agnostic web search abstraction via MCP, implement the AgentQ-style autonomous research loop, and wire web provenance and conflict handling into the audit trail and self-correcting loop.
113	[normal] Phase 8 (Holistic Data Expansion): Light up the remaining facets in order of cost — Operational (extend the Postgres schema with customer/supplier/HR tables), then Legal (onboard a contract corpus via RAPTOR using EDGAR exhibits / CUAD / a simulated data room), then Compliance (add the compliance register paired with CAG rules). Each facet ships behind the data-adaptive contract (Section 10.2), with coverage reporting, before the next is added.
114	[Heading 1] 15. Open Items & Risk Register
115	[normal] The following technical claims from the original draft were left unchanged in this revision but should be pressure-tested before implementation:
116	[normal] Paged Attention fragmentation figure: The original draft cited a reduction from ~60% fragmentation to “near 0%.” The qualitative claim (large reduction) is sound; the specific figures should be replaced with measured numbers from your own hardware rather than stated as fact.
117	[normal] RAGAS threshold values: The faithfulness < 0.85 and answer_relevance < 0.80 cutoffs are placeholders. These should be calibrated against a labeled validation set, not hardcoded on intuition.
118	[normal] RAPTOR leaf size (~300 tokens): A reasonable default, but optimal chunk size for dense financial tables vs. prose differs; worth an ablation.
119	[Heading 1] 16. Glossary
120	[normal] RAPTOR: Recursive Abstractive Processing for Tree-Organized Retrieval — a hierarchical index built by recursively clustering and summarizing text into a tree; used here as the within-node summarize-or-split mechanism.
121	[normal] PageIndex: A vectorless, reasoning-based retrieval approach that builds a table-of-contents tree from a document’s natural structure and uses LLM reasoning to navigate to relevant sections; used here for the structural-navigation phase.
122	[normal] Structure-Routed Hybrid Retrieval: Aegis’s retrieval design — PageIndex-style ToC navigation to the correct node, then within-node RAPTOR (summary for global queries, span search for local queries) to avoid context overflow and needle-in-section loss.
123	[normal] Retrieval Plan / (node, intent) instruction: The set of typed instructions navigation emits for a compound query, each pairing a target node with the granularity its sub-question needs (global summary or local span).
124	[normal] Coverage Check: A verification, ahead of the faithfulness gate, that the assembled retrieved context addresses every sub-question a compound query was decomposed into.
125	[normal] CAG (Cache-Augmented Generation): Serving a fixed corpus by keeping its precomputed KV state resident and reused, instead of retrieving it per query.
126	[normal] KV Cache: The Key/Value vectors computed per token during prefill; caching them avoids recomputation on reuse.
127	[normal] APC (Automatic Prefix Caching): vLLM’s best-effort reuse of KV blocks across requests that share an exact prompt prefix; LRU-evicted.
128	[normal] RadixAttention: SGLang’s prefix-cache mechanism storing KV state in a radix tree, enabling multi-level prefix sharing and fork/branch sharing across all concurrent requests.
129	[normal] PagedAttention: vLLM technique that stores the KV cache in non-contiguous fixed-size pages to eliminate memory fragmentation.
130	[normal] Continuous Batching: Iteration-level scheduling that admits new requests into the running batch at token boundaries to raise throughput.
131	[normal] TTFT: Time-to-first-token — latency from request to the first generated token; reduced by prefix caching.
132	[normal] GGUF: The quantized model file format used by llama.cpp for edge/CPU deployment.
133	[normal] Quantization (Q4_K_M / Q8_0): Reducing weight precision to 4-/8-bit to cut memory use with minimal quality loss.
134	[normal] MCP (Model Context Protocol): Anthropic’s open JSON-RPC protocol letting agents call external tools (e.g., Postgres, Tableau) through a uniform contract.
135	[normal] RAGAS: A framework of metrics (e.g., faithfulness, answer relevance, context recall) used to score and gate RAG output.
136	[normal] GMM (Gaussian Mixture Model): The clustering method RAPTOR uses to group leaf embeddings before summarization.
137	[normal] MNPI: Material Non-Public Information — confidential data whose external transmission is prohibited.
138	[normal] LMCache: A persistent, tiered (GPU→CPU→SSD) KV-cache store used for CAG Tier 2 residency guarantees.
139	[normal] Working / Episodic / Semantic Memory: Active task state / short-term session buffer / long-term durable fact store, respectively.
140	[normal] Memory Compaction: A background process that distills episodic logs into semantic facts and purges redundant transcripts.
141	[normal] Concept / Data Drift: Shifts in document structure, reporting style, or data distribution that may require index rebuilds.
142	[normal] Self-Correcting Loop: A generation cycle wrapped in RAGAS scoring that reformulates and retries when quality is too low.
143	[normal] Supervisor / Specialist Agent: The orchestrating LangGraph node and its delegated domain nodes (Financial, Legal, Forecasting, Data-Tooling).
144	[normal] LangGraph: The framework used to express the hierarchical agent state machine.
145	[normal] Vector DB (Qdrant): The store holding embeddings for RAPTOR leaves and semantic memory.
146	[normal] EDGAR: The SEC’s public system for downloading company filings (10-K / 10-Q).
147	[normal] CDC (Change Data Capture): Detecting new/changed records (files or ledger rows) to trigger event-driven ingestion.
148	[normal] Claims layer vs. Evidence layer: What a filing asserts (claims) versus the granular records that verify it (evidence); auditing is the cross-check of one against the other.
149	[normal] Capability Tiers / Data-Adaptive Operation: The model by which Aegis runs on a minimal data set and progressively enables more diligence facets (Legal, Operational, Compliance) as their evidence layers are added.
150	[normal] Data Room: The secured repository where, in a real transaction, a target company lawfully provides its internal records (including MNPI) to the diligence party under NDA.
151	[normal] CUAD: The Contract Understanding Atticus Dataset — a public, clause-annotated corpus of legal contracts usable as a real proxy for the Legal facet.
152	[normal] Sovereign Model: Aegis’s own fine-tuned, locally-hosted model — the default inference backend.
153	[normal] Provider Abstraction / Operating Modes: A router that makes local engines and external providers interchangeable; Sovereign Mode is local-only, External Mode is governed opt-in.
154	[normal] Data-Classification Gate: Policy layer that blocks confidential/MNPI payloads from external routing, permitting only public/synthetic data to leave the host.
155	[normal] SFT (Supervised / Instruction Fine-Tuning): Training a base model on curated instruction–response pairs to adapt it to task formats.
156	[normal] LoRA / QLoRA: Parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods that train small adapter weights (QLoRA over a quantized base), enabling training on bounded hardware.
157	[normal] Knowledge Distillation (Teacher/Student): Training a smaller student model on the outputs or reasoning traces of a larger teacher model.
158	[normal] DPO (Direct Preference Optimization): Preference-based training on pairs of better/worse responses; used here for multi-step agentic robustness.
159	[normal] MCTS (Monte Carlo Tree Search): A guided search over action sequences balancing exploration and exploitation; used in AgentQ to generate agent trajectories.
160	[normal] AgentQ: A web-agent training framework (MultiOn) combining guided MCTS, AI self-critique, and off-policy DPO for self-healing autonomous agents.
161	[normal] ZDR (Zero Data Retention): A provider mode that purges queries/data after serving, preferred for privacy-sensitive web search.
162	[normal] Deep Research Loop: An iterative search–read–refine–research cycle that gathers and cross-checks many sources before answering.
163	[normal] Exa / Tavily / Firecrawl / Valyu: Agent-native web search/extraction APIs returning clean, cited, structured content; Valyu additionally covers SEC and finance sources.
164	=== TABLES: 2
165	--- TABLE 0 ---
166	Data Source | Modality & Storage | Operational Role | Fidelity Vector
167	SEC Filings (10-K / 10-Q) | Unstructured PDF / HTML inside local Vector DB | Establishes the foundational corporate risk text, lease liabilities, and public declarations (the claims layer for all four facets). | 100% real-world data downloaded directly from SEC EDGAR via programmatic APIs.
168	ERP Financial Ledger | Relational data tables inside local PostgreSQL | Provides individual transaction entries, payroll records, and vendor lines to audit text claims (Accounting evidence). | Simulated via Python Faker, scripted to intentionally match or conflict with the totals declared in the 10-K. Source-agnostic: replaceable by a lawfully-provided real ledger.
169	Regulatory Frameworks | Static Markdown files pinned inside memory cache | Acts as strict regulatory guidelines (e.g., GAAP, IRS Tax Codes) to run verification checks (Compliance rules). | 100% real-world legal definitions compiled from regulatory repositories.
170	BI Configurations | JSON metadata schemas via Tableau MCP | Controls the target configurations of internal corporate reporting dashboards. | Locally defined JSON payloads mapped to relational database views.
171	Legal Document Corpus (progressive) | Unstructured contracts / leases / IP / dockets; structure-routed retrieval (ToC navigation + within-node RAPTOR) | Clause-level evidence for the Legal facet — change-of-control, indemnities, exclusivity, IP ownership, litigation exposure. | Real public proxies (EDGAR material-contract exhibits, the CUAD annotated-contract dataset, court dockets) plus a simulated data room with seeded clauses.
172	Operational Data Tables (progressive) | Relational tables inside local PostgreSQL | Granular evidence for the Operational facet — customer concentration/churn, supplier concentration, headcount/attrition, unit economics. | Simulated extension of the Postgres schema (customer/CRM, supplier, HR), optionally seeded with landmines (e.g., revenue concentration).
173	Compliance Register (progressive) | Relational table / structured records in PostgreSQL | Evidence of actual compliance status for the Compliance facet — licenses, permits, certifications, enforcement/violation history — paired with CAG rules. | Simulated register plus optional real public enforcement databases; seeded past violations to test detection.
174	--- TABLE 1 ---
175	Base vs. bar | Gap to provider | Diagnosis | Action
176	Clears bar | Small | Adequate | Ship base locally — stop.
177	Clears bar | Large | Adequate but behind frontier | Usually ship base; train only if the gap materially helps the use case and lifecycle cost is justified.
178	Fails bar | Provider also weak | Retrieval / data problem | Fix RAPTOR / CAG / prompting; do not train.
179	Fails bar | Provider clears it | Genuine capability gap | Train; use the provider as the distillation teacher.
